incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a production CI/CD pipeline dependent on GitHub Actions you should activate a fallback build path (self-hosted runners or alternative platform) and audit any deployments that failed silently between 10:57 and 13:18 UTC on May 26 within the next 24-72 hours.
incidents.emerging_risk_signal: As AI coding agents rely on GitHub Actions for automated PR validation, authentication-layer outages now simultaneously blind both human and machine software delivery pipelines, compounding systemic delivery risk.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must document the specific authentication service component
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's engineering team must produce a written architectural fault map identifying the specific authentication service component whose failure at 10:57 UTC on May 26, 2026 caused Actions workflow initiation and action dependency downloads to fail globally, naming the component, its role in the runner job dispatch path, and any single-point-of-failure topology. Within 30 days of that documentation, GitHub must implement and evidence architectural redundancy for that component — such as active-active replication, independent fallback authentication nodes, or circuit-breaker isolation — such that the loss of a single instance cannot reproduce the May 26 global failure scope. Completion evidence must include the fault map, a revised architecture diagram, and a test result demonstrating that the redundant path serves authentication requests when the primary component is taken offline.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires redundancy planning in the abstract; this adaptation anchors the obligation to the confirmed 10:57 UTC onset of the May 26 auth failure and the specific Actions runner dispatch and dependency download paths that were severed, preventing GitHub from satisfying the control with generic infrastructure improvements unrelated to the actual failure point.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must define an SLO breach threshold for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must define a measurable SLO breach threshold — expressed as authentication error rate percentage and/or p99 latency — for the Actions authentication service that, when crossed, automatically triggers failover or load-shedding before the system reaches the majority-of-runs failure state confirmed at 11:53 UTC on May 26, 2026; the threshold must be calibrated so that it would have fired during the 10:57–11:53 UTC window, giving at least 30 minutes of automated response time. The threshold definition, the automated failover logic, and evidence that the trigger would have activated during the May 26 signal window must be reviewed and approved by GitHub's Site Reliability leadership within 45 days of the post-incident review publication. Completion evidence consists of the documented SLO definition, the automated runbook or policy it invokes, and a replay test or simulation demonstrating trigger activation against the May 26 telemetry or a synthetic equivalent.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses SLO design broadly; this adaptation uses the confirmed 56-minute gap between the 10:57 UTC onset and the 11:53 UTC majority-failure confirmation as the specific calibration target, requiring the threshold to be sensitive enough to have intervened within that window rather than merely existing on paper.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must validate that the May 26 authentication
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must conduct a structured observability gap review using the May 26, 2026 incident timeline as the test case, specifically validating whether its monitoring and alerting stack would have detected the Actions authentication failure within 5 minutes of the 10:57 UTC onset — i.e., by 11:02 UTC — and comparing that against the actual detection or acknowledgement marker reflected in the githubstatus.com update sequence. Where the review reveals that detection lagged beyond 11:02 UTC, GitHub must implement alert rule changes, synthetic probe additions, or log-based anomaly detections targeting the authentication validation path for Actions runner dispatch and Pages request handling, with each new or modified alert backtested against the May 26 signal data. Completion evidence must include the gap review report, the specific alert changes made, and backtest results demonstrating sub-5-minute detection against the May 26 authentication failure signal.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires observability validation in general terms; this adaptation establishes the concrete benchmark of 11:02 UTC (10:57 + 5 minutes) derived from the confirmed incident onset and uses the actual May 26 detection timeline as the measurement datum, ensuring the validation exercise is judged against a real incident signal rather than a hypothetical scenario.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must apply infrastructure hardening specifically to the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Once the post-incident root cause analysis for the May 26, 2026 outage identifies the specific code, configuration, or infrastructure component in the authentication validation path responsible for Actions runner job dispatch failures and action dependency download failures, GitHub's infrastructure security team must apply targeted hardening measures to that path — including at minimum: input validation hardening, credential or token validation retry limits, dependency isolation to prevent cascading failure propagation, and removal of any shared-fate coupling between the Actions auth path and the GitHub Pages availability layer confirmed to have degraded concurrently. Hardening measures must be documented in a security control record referencing the May 26 root cause findings and validated via a penetration or fault-injection test of the hardened path within 60 days of the post-incident review publication. If the root cause analysis is not published within 5 business days as required, the hardening scope must default to all components identified in the incident timeline pending final confirmation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for infrastructure hardening broadly; this adaptation scopes hardening specifically to the authentication validation path for runner job dispatch and dependency downloads as the confirmed failure surface, and adds the contingency that hardening must begin against the confirmed interim scope if the RCA is delayed, preventing the control from being blocked by the as-yet-unpublished root cause analysis.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must implement canary-based deployment validation for changes
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must implement a canary deployment policy for all changes to the Actions authentication infrastructure such that any future modification to the authentication validation service — of the type that precipitated the May 26, 2026 global outage — is staged through a canary cohort representing no more than 5% of global Actions workflow traffic before full rollout, with automated rollback triggered if authentication error rates in the canary cohort exceed a defined threshold within a defined observation window. The policy must define the canary traffic percentage, the error-rate rollback threshold, the observation window duration, and the change categories subject to mandatory canary gating, and must explicitly cover the class of change that caused the May 26 failure once identified in the post-incident review. Completion evidence includes the published canary deployment policy, evidence of its enforcement in the CI/CD pipeline for the authentication service, and a test deployment demonstrating automated rollback on synthetic error injection.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires canary validation for infrastructure changes; this adaptation ties the policy scope specifically to the Actions authentication infrastructure and mandates that the change category responsible for the May 26 global-scope failure be explicitly enumerated in the canary gating rules, closing the gap that allowed a single deployment to affect all global Actions workflow initiations simultaneously.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must maintain and exercise an incident response
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub's incident response team must maintain a dedicated runbook for authentication-layer failures that simultaneously affect both GitHub Actions and GitHub Pages — the joint failure mode confirmed on May 26, 2026 — with the runbook containing: explicit triage steps for authentication service degradation in the Actions runner dispatch path, escalation paths active during UTC business hours and off-hours, coordination procedures between the Actions SRE and Pages SRE teams, and a target resolution time of less than 2.4 hours benchmarked against the confirmed 10:57–13:18 UTC duration of the May 26 incident. The runbook must be exercised in a tabletop or live simulation at least once within 90 days of the May 26 post-incident review publication, with the simulation scenario seeded from the confirmed May 26 failure mode. Completion evidence includes the runbook document with version history, the exercise completion record, and a gap closure log from lessons identified during the exercise.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires runbook maintenance for operational incidents; this adaptation specifies the joint Actions-and-Pages failure mode as the mandatory scenario type and anchors the RTO target to the confirmed 2.4-hour May 26 outage duration, preventing the runbook from being drafted against a less demanding scenario that would not meet the benchmark set by this specific incident.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must publish a full post-incident review for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must publish a full post-incident review for the May 26, 2026 authentication outage on a publicly accessible channel within 5 business days of the 13:18 UTC resolution, covering at minimum: the specific root cause of the authentication service failure that began at 10:57 UTC, contributing factors that caused the joint impact to both GitHub Actions workflow initiation and GitHub Pages availability, the timeline from onset through detection to resolution, and committed corrective actions with owners and target dates. A designated GitHub communications owner must be identified for this deliverable within 24 hours of resolution, and the draft must be reviewed by technical leadership before publication. Completion evidence is the published post-incident review URL, the publication timestamp demonstrating the 5-business-day deadline was met, and a record of the review approval by technical leadership.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires post-incident review publication; this adaptation specifies the exact content requirements — root cause, contributing factors to the joint Actions/Pages impact, the confirmed onset-to-resolution timeline, and owner-linked corrective actions — ensuring the published review substantively addresses the confirmed incident facts rather than producing a high-level status summary that does not advance accountability.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must test a fallback execution path for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must design, document, and validate a fallback execution path for GitHub Actions that allows self-hosted runners already registered to an enterprise or organisation to continue processing queued workflow jobs when the cloud-hosted authentication service is in a degraded state equivalent to the one confirmed on May 26, 2026 — specifically the state where new workflow initiations and action dependency downloads fail due to authentication validation breakdown. The fallback path must be tested in a staging environment using a simulated authentication service outage of at least 2.4 hours' duration, with test results documenting which workflow categories continued, which were queued for retry, and what the runner registration mechanism fell back to. Completion evidence includes the fallback architecture document, the test plan, and test results demonstrating continuity of self-hosted runner job processing under simulated May 26-equivalent auth degradation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses operational continuity during service degradation; this adaptation specifies the exact degradation mode — authentication validation failure preventing workflow initiation and dependency downloads, as confirmed on May 26 — and requires the fallback test to use a simulated outage matching the confirmed 2.4-hour duration, ensuring the validated path is calibrated to the real incident's scope rather than a shorter or less severe test scenario.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must define a maximum acceptable degradation window
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must formally document its maximum acceptable degradation window for GitHub Actions availability — expressed as a maximum continuous outage duration — and must assess within 10 business days of the May 26, 2026 resolution whether the confirmed 2.4-hour global outage (10:57–13:18 UTC) breached that defined threshold; if it did, GitHub must initiate a formal SLA credit review for affected enterprise customers and a corrective-action review with named owners and completion dates. The degradation window definition, the breach determination, the SLA credit review outcome, and the corrective-action register must each be documented and shared with enterprise customer account teams. Completion evidence includes the documented degradation window policy, the formal breach determination memo referencing the May 26 confirmed timeline, and evidence of SLA credit review initiation or a documented rationale for why no credit applies under existing contract terms.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires SLA threshold definition and breach review; this adaptation requires the threshold to be explicitly assessed against the confirmed 2.4-hour May 26 outage duration and links the breach determination directly to SLA credit review initiation, preventing the threshold definition exercise from being decoupled from the specific incident that triggered the obligation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Enterprise customers dependent on GitHub Actions for production
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Enterprise customers that relied on GitHub Actions as their sole or primary CI/CD execution platform and experienced build or deployment pipeline failures during the May 26, 2026 outage (10:57–13:18 UTC) must conduct a third-party concentration risk assessment within 30 days of the post-incident review publication, documenting the number and criticality of production deployments blocked during the 2.4-hour window and identifying at least one alternative execution path — such as a self-hosted runner infrastructure, a secondary CI/CD provider, or cached artifact promotion workflows — for pipelines classified as critical-path. The assessment must be owned by the enterprise's platform or DevOps leadership and reviewed by the CISO or technology risk function. Completion evidence includes the concentration risk assessment document, the list of critical pipelines reviewed, the documented alternative execution path for each, and evidence of CISO or risk function sign-off.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control addresses third-party concentration risk broadly; this adaptation anchors the reassessment obligation to the confirmed May 26 production CI/CD disruption and requires the alternative pipeline documentation to be scoped specifically to pipelines that were affected during the confirmed 2.4-hour outage window, making the exercise actionable against a real impact inventory rather than a hypothetical dependency audit.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Enterprise customers must review contractual SLA terms with
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Enterprise customers with GitHub enterprise agreements covering GitHub Actions availability must, within 15 business days of the May 26, 2026 resolution, conduct a formal review of their contractual SLA terms with GitHub to determine whether the confirmed 2.4-hour global Actions outage (10:57–13:18 UTC on May 26, 2026) meets the definition of a service credit event or a material breach under their agreement, and must document that determination with supporting contract citations. Where a credit or remediation right is identified, the enterprise must submit a formal claim or escalation to GitHub within the contractual notice window. Completion evidence includes the contract review memo with relevant SLA clause citations, the breach/no-breach determination, and — where applicable — evidence of a formal credit claim submission to GitHub.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires contractual SLA review after a third-party service event; this adaptation specifies the confirmed 2.4-hour outage window as the measurement input for the breach determination and adds the notice-period compliance requirement for claim submission, ensuring the review produces an actionable legal outcome rather than a passive documentation exercise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must issue a stakeholder-facing incident summary addressing
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must issue a structured stakeholder-facing incident summary for the May 26, 2026 authentication outage within 48 hours of the 13:18 UTC resolution — i.e., by 13:18 UTC on May 28, 2026 — directed at enterprise customers and distributed through a channel beyond githubstatus.com, such as direct email to enterprise account contacts or the GitHub enterprise customer portal. The summary must address: the confirmed impact on GitHub Actions workflow initiation and action dependency downloads, the concurrent degradation of GitHub Pages, the confirmed onset and resolution times, and the expected publication date of the full post-incident review. A named communications owner responsible for drafting and distributing the summary must be identified within 4 hours of resolution. Completion evidence includes the distributed summary document, the distribution channel record with timestamp, and confirmation that the 48-hour deadline was met.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires post-incident stakeholder communication; this adaptation specifies the 48-hour absolute deadline derived from the confirmed 13:18 UTC resolution, mandates a channel beyond the githubstatus.com status page that enterprise customers impacted by the May 26 CI/CD disruption would actually receive, and requires the summary to reference the confirmed joint Actions and Pages impact rather than a generic service disruption description.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: GitHub must proactively communicate the anticipated post-incident review
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: GitHub must proactively communicate to enterprise customers who experienced GitHub Actions build and deployment failures on May 26, 2026 — within 48 hours of incident resolution — a specific committed publication date for the full post-incident review, acknowledging that the root cause analysis had not been published at the time of resolution notice and providing the enterprise account team's contact for follow-up. This communication must be issued through a direct enterprise customer channel (not githubstatus.com alone) and must be followed by a confirmation message once the post-incident review is published, including the review URL. Completion evidence includes the outbound communication record with timestamps, the list of enterprise accounts notified, the committed review publication date stated in the communication, and the follow-up confirmation sent upon publication.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires proactive communication about post-incident review timelines; this adaptation is grounded in the confirmed fact that the root cause analysis had not been published at disclosure time and requires GitHub to bridge that gap with a specific committed date rather than an open-ended acknowledgement, directly addressing the information uncertainty that enterprise customers operating impacted CI/CD pipelines would face following the May 26 outage.

incidents.headline: AWS multi-service increased error rates persist across May 23-26
incidents.summary: Amazon Web Services recorded a sustained 'Increased Error Rates' warn-level incident beginning May 23, 2026 at 00:15 UTC that carried into the sweep window, with StatusGator logging the warning state active for 13 hours and 37 minutes on May 26 alone. User-reported symptoms on May 26 included AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) sign-in failures across multiple organisations and geographies, Route 53 hosted-zone lookup errors, Lightsail connectivity failures, and AWS Bedrock connectivity failures in the Europe (Paris) region, with the AWS Health Dashboard (event ID BA540) recording the multi-service operational issue from May 25 into May 26. The incident represented the tail of a nearly three-day degradation event, compounding an earlier 13-day warn-period for increased error rates that began May 9, indicating a pattern of persistent service quality erosion across AWS infrastructure components.

secondary_mappings.why: Multi-day persistence of error rates across diverse AWS services (SSO, Route53, Bedrock, Lightsail) points to a systemic infrastructure resilience deficit rather than an isolated component fault.
Downstream enterprise customers relying on AWS IAM Identity Center and Route53 for authentication and DNS experienced operational disruptions affecting business-critical workloads.
The breadth of affected services (identity, DNS, database, compute) across a nearly three-day window illustrates the systemic concentration risk of hyperscaler dependency.

incidents.severity_rationale: Sustained warn-level degradation across multiple AWS services spanning the window, with real user-reported authentication and connectivity failures, but no confirmed full service outage or quantified financial impact; severity capped at 2 as AWS did not escalate to a full outage declaration.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Instrument automated detection for simultaneous warn-state across AWS
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Configure automated, correlated monitoring across AWS IAM Identity Center, Route 53, and Bedrock eu-west-3 such that any combination of warn-state signals from two or more of these services within a single 15-minute polling window triggers an P1 escalation alert to the on-call engineering team within 30 minutes of first detection — eliminating the structural gap evidenced by the 13 hours and 37 minutes of unresponded warn-state logged by StatusGator on May 26, 2026. The monitoring pipeline must ingest AWS Health Dashboard events (including BA540-class multi-service records) and third-party status sources (StatusGator, IsDown) as independent signal feeds, with alerting logic that does not depend solely on AWS-native tooling. Evidence of completion is a documented runbook entry, a successful synthetic test firing the correlated alert, and a dated sign-off from the Head of Platform Engineering.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires multi-service detection instrumentation; this adaptation anchors the 30-minute escalation SLA to the specific 13-hour-37-minute undetected window on May 26 and mandates dual-source signal ingestion to address the structural dependency on AWS-native health reporting that failed to prompt timely internal escalation during event BA540.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Maintain and test multi-region failover for AWS IAM
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Validate and maintain a tested multi-region failover procedure for AWS IAM Identity Center SSO that includes at least one set of break-glass IAM user credentials stored outside the Identity Center control plane, capable of granting console and API access within 15 minutes of Identity Center unavailability, with the procedure explicitly validated against the SIGNIN_BAD_REQUEST_ERROR failure mode reported across multiple organisations and geographies on May 26, 2026. A tabletop exercise must be conducted within 30 days simulating a complete IAM Identity Center outage of the duration observed during the May 23-26 event (up to 72+ hours), with break-glass credentials confirmed functional and recovery steps documented step-by-step for on-call responders. Evidence of completion is a post-exercise report signed by the CISO confirming that all critical AWS workloads could be accessed via break-glass credentials without dependency on the Identity Center sign-in flow.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires multi-region SSO failover capability; this adaptation targets the specific SIGNIN_BAD_REQUEST_ERROR failure mode and the multi-day duration of the May 23-26 event to ensure break-glass procedures are calibrated to real failure conditions rather than shorter theoretical outage windows.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Define and enforce error-rate SLO breach thresholds for
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Define explicit SLO breach thresholds for AWS Route 53 hosted-zone resolution — specifically including failures of Terraform data-source lookups against hosted zones — such that a sustained error rate or lookup failure exceeding 5% over any 5-minute window triggers an automated escalation alert and initiates a pre-approved failover procedure to an alternative DNS resolver (e.g., self-managed BIND, Cloudflare DNS, or Route 53 Resolver in an unaffected region), with the threshold calibrated against the degradation pattern documented in the May 23-26 incident. The failover initiation procedure must be tested quarterly using synthetic failures against Route 53 hosted-zone resolution and must produce an automated incident ticket referencing the BA540 event class as a severity benchmark. Evidence of completion is a documented SLO threshold definition approved by the Head of Infrastructure, a passing quarterly test report, and an alert-to-ticket automation record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires Route 53 error-rate SLO enforcement; this adaptation specifies the Terraform data-source lookup failure mode reported on May 26, sets a concrete percentage threshold calibrated to the incident, and mandates a pre-approved failover path rather than leaving escalation as the terminal action.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish recovery time objectives for AWS Bedrock eu-west-3
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Establish and document Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) for AWS Bedrock inference endpoints in eu-west-3 (Europe Paris) that are explicitly stress-tested against a degradation scenario of at least 72 continuous hours — matching the nearly three-day duration of the May 23-26 incident — with validated automatic fallback routing to Bedrock eu-west-1 or a designated alternative inference provider (e.g., Azure OpenAI Service or a self-hosted model endpoint) activated within 60 minutes of confirmed eu-west-3 unavailability. A failover drill must be completed within 45 days, simulating total loss of Bedrock eu-west-3 availability for a 72-hour window, with all AI/ML-dependent workloads confirmed operational via the fallback route within the defined RTO. Evidence of completion is a signed drill report from the Head of AI/ML Engineering documenting RTO achievement, latency metrics from the fallback route, and updated architecture diagrams reflecting the eu-west-3 to eu-west-1 or alternative-provider routing path.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires Bedrock RTO definition; this adaptation anchors the test duration to the actual 72-hour-plus degradation window of the May 23-26 event and sets a concrete 60-minute activation SLA for fallback routing rather than leaving RTO values unspecified.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Map all AWS infrastructure dependencies that traverse both
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Produce a documented dependency map of all AWS infrastructure components that simultaneously traverse the Route 53 DNS resolution plane and the IAM Identity Center authentication plane within the organisation's AWS environment, explicitly identifying shared control-plane components or regional endpoints whose failure could produce the correlated multi-service cascade observed from May 23-26, 2026 (event BA540). The map must annotate each dependency with its single-point-of-failure risk, identify which workloads lost access when both planes degraded concurrently, and propose architectural decoupling or compensating controls for any dependency rated high-risk, with findings reviewed by the Head of Architecture within 60 days. Evidence of completion is a dated dependency map artefact in the organisation's configuration management system, a risk-rated findings register, and a signed review record from the Head of Architecture.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires AWS dependency mapping; this adaptation specifically targets the shared-component coupling between Route 53 and IAM Identity Center that produced the multi-service cascade during BA540, directing the map to surface the architectural conditions that made simultaneous failure of DNS and authentication planes possible.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct capacity and resilience testing for AWS IAM
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Design and execute a resilience test for AWS IAM Identity Center that simulates degraded Route 53 conditions — specifically injecting hosted-zone resolution failures or elevated lookup latency consistent with the error-rate profile reported during May 23-26, 2026 — and validates whether IAM Identity Center SSO sign-in succeeds, degrades gracefully, or produces the SIGNIN_BAD_REQUEST_ERROR failure mode reported across multiple organisations on May 26. The test must be run in a non-production AWS environment within 60 days, with results documenting the failure threshold at which SIGNIN_BAD_REQUEST_ERROR is reproducible, and must inform updates to the break-glass credential procedure (referenced in AC-TEC-012-OUT-01) if Route 53 degradation is confirmed as a contributing trigger. Evidence of completion is a test execution report approved by the Head of Platform Engineering, including failure reproduction data, and a record of any resulting updates to incident response runbooks.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires resilience testing of IAM Identity Center under degraded conditions; this adaptation specifically targets the causal coupling between Route 53 hosted-zone resolution failures and the SIGNIN_BAD_REQUEST_ERROR outcome to generate empirical data on the failure mechanism observed during the May 26 multi-organisation impact.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update the business continuity plan to address AWS
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Update the Business Continuity Plan (BCP) within 30 days to include a dedicated procedure for AWS Health Dashboard BA540-class incidents — defined as multi-service warn-state events lasting more than 72 hours and affecting IAM Identity Center, Route 53, and/or Bedrock simultaneously — specifying manual-access procedures that bypass IAM Identity Center SSO (including break-glass IAM user activation steps), communication escalation paths to AWS Enterprise Support, and decision criteria for invoking alternative-provider fallback for DNS and inference workloads. The updated BCP section must reference the May 23-26, 2026 incident as the calibrating scenario, define the 72-hour mark as the trigger for escalating to executive crisis management, and be approved by the Chief Operating Officer and CISO. Evidence of completion is a version-controlled BCP document with a dated approval record and a completed read-through acknowledgement from all BCP-designated personnel.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires BCP coverage of multi-service AWS incidents; this adaptation defines the BA540-class incident profile as a named BCP scenario, sets 72 hours as the explicit escalation trigger calibrated to the May 23-26 duration, and requires bypass procedures for IAM Identity Center specifically — addressing the authentication dependency that would otherwise block manual recovery actions.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate third-party incident management protocols within four hours
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Establish and publish an internal escalation protocol requiring that third-party incident management procedures — including notification to AWS Enterprise Support, engagement of the organisation's AWS Technical Account Manager, and internal P1 incident bridge activation — are initiated within four hours of the AWS Health Dashboard first recording a warn-state for any combination of IAM Identity Center, Route 53, or Bedrock, anchored to the operational gap identified on May 23, 2026 at 00:15 UTC when the current incident began without confirmed prompt internal escalation. The protocol must designate a named on-call role accountable for monitoring AWS Health Dashboard and third-party trackers (StatusGator, IsDown) and must define the four-hour clock as starting from the earliest of: AWS Health Dashboard warn-state onset, StatusGator warn-state log, or IsDown outage record — whichever occurs first. Evidence of completion is a published escalation protocol document, a named role assignment record, and a tabletop test confirming the four-hour activation window is achievable.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires third-party incident protocol activation; this adaptation fixes the four-hour clock to the earliest of multiple monitoring source signals (not solely AWS Health Dashboard) and names the May 23 00:15 UTC onset as the calibrating reference point, closing the delayed-escalation gap documented during the BA540 event.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Test and document RTO compliance for AWS-dependent workloads
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 45 days, conduct a structured RTO compliance review for all AWS-dependent business workloads by simulating a 13-hour continuous warn-state degradation scenario modelled on the May 26, 2026 profile — during which IAM Identity Center SSO, Route 53 hosted-zone resolution, and Bedrock eu-west-3 are simultaneously impaired but not fully unavailable — and confirm that each workload either remains within its documented impact-tolerance threshold throughout the 13-hour window or has a documented compensating control (failover, manual procedure, or alternative provider) that restores it to within tolerance within its stated RTO. Any workload found out of tolerance must be escalated to the relevant business owner with a remediation plan due within 30 days of the review. Evidence of completion is a workload-by-workload RTO compliance register, a list of out-of-tolerance findings with assigned owners, and a sign-off from the Chief Operating Officer.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires RTO testing against warn-state scenarios; this adaptation calibrates the test duration precisely to the 13-hour-37-minute warn-state window logged on May 26 and specifies the degraded-but-not-fully-unavailable service condition — the actual failure mode of BA540 — rather than a clean total-outage scenario that would overstate recovery difficulty.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update Amazon Web Services' third-party risk record to
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Update Amazon Web Services' third-party risk record in the organisation's vendor risk register within 15 days to document the cumulative degradation pattern spanning May 9 through May 26, 2026 — comprising a 13-day initial warn-period beginning May 9 followed by a nearly three-day multi-service incident from May 23 (IsDown outage start: May 22, 'Increased API Error Rates') through May 26, totalling a 17-day window of persistent service quality erosion — and escalate the AWS reliability risk rating by at least one tier pending receipt and review of a root-cause analysis for event BA540. The updated record must include a formal assessment of whether AWS's observed availability profile over this 17-day window constitutes a breach of contractual SLA commitments, with a recommendation to the Chief Procurement Officer on whether enhanced monitoring, contract renegotiation, or concentration-risk mitigation is warranted. Evidence of completion is a dated vendor risk register update, an SLA breach assessment memo, and a signed recommendation record addressed to the Chief Procurement Officer.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires third-party risk record updates; this adaptation captures the compounding structure of the May 9-26 degradation pattern as a single 17-day evidence artefact and triggers a formal SLA breach assessment rather than treating the update as a passive documentation exercise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Require AWS to provide a written root-cause analysis
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Issue a formal written request to Amazon Web Services via the Enterprise Support channel within 10 business days demanding a root-cause analysis (RCA) for Health Dashboard event ID BA540, with a response deadline of 30 days from request date, requiring the RCA to specifically address: (a) the mechanism by which Route 53 DNS resolution degradation coupled with IAM Identity Center failures to produce multi-geography SSO errors on May 26, 2026; (b) the sequence of service degradation from the May 23, 2026 00:15 UTC onset through the May 26 warn-state; and (c) AWS's remediation actions and any architectural changes made to prevent recurrence. If AWS does not provide an adequate RCA within the 30-day window, the organisation's vendor risk escalation process must be triggered, including notification to the Chief Procurement Officer and escalation of the AWS risk rating. Evidence of completion is a copy of the RCA request with submission timestamp, the received RCA document (or a record of non-response triggering escalation), and the escalation decision record if applicable.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires an RCA request from AWS; this adaptation specifies the exact technical questions the RCA must answer — Route 53-to-Identity-Center coupling mechanism and multi-geography failure propagation — and attaches a non-response escalation consequence to prevent the request from being treated as a passive formality.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Reassess concentration risk thresholds for AWS as a
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Commission a concentration risk assessment for AWS as the organisation's sole-source provider for both IAM identity services (Identity Center) and DNS resolution (Route 53) within 30 days, using the May 23-26, 2026 incident as the primary stress scenario — in which simultaneous failure of both foundational planes across multiple regions produced compounded access failures — and evaluate whether the organisation's current concentration thresholds for a single cloud provider controlling both the authentication and name-resolution planes are acceptable given the demonstrated correlated failure risk. The assessment must quantify the blast radius of a recurrence (workloads impacted, revenue or operational exposure per hour of joint degradation), evaluate at least two architectural alternatives for decoupling DNS or identity from exclusive AWS dependency (e.g., Okta or Azure AD for identity, Cloudflare or self-managed DNS for resolution), and present findings to the Board Risk Committee within 45 days with a recommended concentration limit. Evidence of completion is a dated concentration risk assessment report, a Board Risk Committee presentation record, and a documented decision on the recommended concentration threshold.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires concentration risk reassessment; this adaptation uses the May 23-26 event to define the specific correlated failure scenario — simultaneous loss of both the DNS and authentication planes from a single provider — and requires the assessment to produce a Board-level concentration limit decision rather than stopping at documentation.

incidents.headline: Russia fires Oreshnik missile at Kyiv, orders diplomat evacuation
incidents.summary: On 24 May 2026, Russia launched one of its largest attacks on Kyiv since 2022, deploying 90 missiles and 600 drones including an Oreshnik hypersonic ballistic missile, killing at least four people and injuring over 90. On 25-26 May, the Russian Foreign Ministry formally warned all foreign nationals and diplomatic staff to leave Kyiv 'as soon as possible', with FM Lavrov personally calling US Secretary of State Rubio to advise the evacuation of US embassy personnel. The attack and evacuation order, disclosed on 26 May 2026, represent a severe escalation of the Russia-Ukraine conflict with direct implications for diplomatic presence, supply chains through Ukraine, and European security posture.

secondary_mappings.why: Forced evacuation of diplomatic missions from a capital city constitutes a severe political-stability event disrupting international governance and bilateral relations.

The hypersonic Oreshnik missile strike on civilian infrastructure (apartment buildings, marketplace, water supply) mandates immediate physical-security and continuity reviews for all organisations operating in or near Kyiv.
Diplomat evacuation orders and ongoing strikes require business-continuity activation for firms with staff or operations in Ukraine.
The direct Lavrov-Rubio call and EU refusal to evacuate missions creates material stakeholder-communication obligations for any entity with Ukraine exposure.

incidents.severity_rationale: Deployment of a hypersonic ballistic missile against a capital city, combined with an unprecedented formal demand for all NATO-aligned diplomats to evacuate, signals the highest level of conflict escalation in the window.

graded_claims.claim: On 24 May 2026, Russia launched 90 missiles and 600 drones at Ukraine in one of its largest attacks on Kyiv since 2022.
The attack included an Oreshnik hypersonic ballistic missile, deployed for the third time in the four-year war.
The Oreshnik struck the city of Bila Tserkva in the Kyiv region, confirmed by President Zelenskyy.
At least four people were killed across Ukraine and over 90 were injured in the attack.
On 25 May, the Russian Foreign Ministry warned all foreign nationals and diplomats to leave Kyiv 'as soon as possible'.
FM Lavrov personally called US Secretary of State Rubio on 26 May to advise evacuation of US embassy personnel from Kyiv.
The US State Department said there are 'no changes' to its embassy operations in Kyiv as of 26 May.
European countries rejected Russia's evacuation call, vowing to stay in Kyiv and summoning Russian ambassadors in protest.
Putin claimed the attack was retaliation for a Ukrainian strike on a vocational school in Starobilsk, Luhansk, that killed 21 people.
Ukraine disputed Putin's claim, saying it only targeted military facilities.
Russia announced plans for 'systematic and sequential strikes' on Kyiv targeting UAV design, manufacturing and programming facilities.
The Oreshnik is capable of carrying nuclear or conventional warheads and its speed makes it nearly unstoppable by Ukraine's air defenses.

incidents.headline: US strikes southern Iran amid ceasefire, Hormuz shipping disrupted
incidents.summary: On 25 May 2026 (US Memorial Day), US forces conducted 'self-defense strikes' targeting Iranian missile launch sites and boats around the Strait of Hormuz while a ceasefire remained nominally in effect; Iran's foreign ministry on 26 May denounced the strikes as a ceasefire violation and threatened retaliation. The UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre separately reported an explosion aboard a tanker in the Gulf of Oman on 26 May. The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed or severely restricted since late February 2026 when the US-Israel war against Iran began, stranding hundreds of ships and disrupting roughly 20% of global seaborne oil trade and fertiliser supply chains.
incidents.entity: Global commercial shipping / Strait of Hormuz transit operators

secondary_mappings.why: Closure of the Strait of Hormuz disrupts approximately 20% of global seaborne oil trade and fertiliser supply chains, constituting a direct trade-route restriction of systemic magnitude.
US naval blockade of Iranian ports since April 2026 and frozen Iranian asset negotiations are live sanctions instruments intertwined with the military ceasefire dynamics.
Major container and tanker operators (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd) suspended Hormuz transits; ongoing attacks on vessels in the Gulf of Oman further constrain supply-chain routing options.
Iran's IRGC has boarded and attacked merchant vessels in the strait, and a tanker explosion was reported on 26 May, representing active physical seizure and destruction of commercial assets.
Iran's new 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority' toll regime and transit-authorisation requirements impose de-facto regulatory barriers on international shipping.

incidents.severity_rationale: Active US strikes on Iranian territory during a nominal ceasefire, combined with a tanker explosion and the sustained closure of a chokepoint carrying ~20% of global seaborne oil, constitutes a systemic macro shock of the highest severity.
incidents.threat_actor: Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) / Iranian Armed Forces

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure the organisation maintains operational continuity plans specifically calibrated to state-actor-imposed physical closure of the Strait of Hormuz, accounting for the post-February 2026 near-total transit blockage and the May 25 US ceasefire-violation strikes.

Maintain sanctions and geopolitical compliance posture covering both the US naval blockade of Iranian ports (declared April 13, 2026) and IRGC-imposed toll and transit protocols operating north of Larak Island.

Ensure trade-route continuity planning accounts for the structural loss of Hormuz as a viable transit corridor since late February 2026, including pipeline diversion ceilings and Cape of Good Hope rerouting cost modelling.

Ensure operational resilience plans address the physical stranding of vessels and crew in the Persian Gulf — with up to 2,000 vessels and 20,000 seafarers reported immobilised — as a multi-month operational crisis requiring crew welfare and vessel maintenance protocols.

Ensure physical asset protection and voyage risk assessment frameworks are calibrated to the active kinetic threat environment in the Gulf of Oman and Strait of Hormuz following the May 25 strikes and UKMTO-reported tanker explosion on May 26.

graded_claims.claim: US forces conducted strikes targeting Iranian missile launch sites and boats around the Strait of Hormuz on 25 May 2026 while a ceasefire was nominally in effect.
Iran's foreign ministry on 26 May denounced the strikes as a ceasefire violation and threatened retaliation.
UKMTO reported an explosion aboard a tanker in the Gulf of Oman on 26 May 2026.
The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed or severely restricted since late February 2026 when the US-Israel war against Iran began.
The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly 20% of global seaborne oil trade.
Up to 20,000 seafarers have been stranded on some 2,000 vessels in the Persian Gulf.
Crude and condensate loadings fell approximately 74% from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026 (16.6M to 4.3M bpd).
Hapag-Lloyd was 'currently refraining' from transiting the Strait of Hormuz as of early April 2026.
War-risk ship insurance premiums for the strait increased from 0.125% to between 0.2% and 0.4% of ship insurance value per transit.
More than 400 oil-laden tankers and dozens of LNG/LPG carriers remained anchored outside the Gulf in early April 2026 awaiting passage signals.
Transit volumes through Hormuz fell 97% following Operation Epic Fury and IRGC closure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update the geopolitical risk scenario library by 30
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 May 2026, the Chief Risk Officer must formally amend the geopolitical risk scenario library to reclassify the 25 May 2026 US CENTCOM Memorial Day strikes on Iranian missile launch sites and IRGC boats as a confirmed ceasefire-violation event, replacing any prior 'nominal ceasefire intact' assumption; the updated library must re-baseline Strait of Hormuz transit-disruption probability to greater than 90% for Q3 2026 planning, citing the confirmed 97% transit volume collapse post-Operation Epic Fury and the confirmed closure since late February 2026 as supporting evidence. The updated scenario entry must be version-controlled in the risk library system, signed off by the CRO, and distributed to all commercial, treasury, and logistics planning teams before 30 May 2026 close of business. Evidence of completion is the dated, signed scenario library entry showing the revised probability baseline and the distribution log to affected teams.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires scenario library maintenance; this adaptation locks in the specific ceasefire-violation reclassification trigger, the 90%+ Q3 probability threshold, and the evidentiary chain from confirmed 97% transit collapse to the re-baselined figure — none of which a generic geopolitical risk update would specify.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate escalation protocols for the Strait of Hormuz zone
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: With immediate effect from 26 May 2026, the Head of Maritime Security must formally activate Level 5 (active state-actor kinetic threat) escalation protocols for the Strait of Hormuz zone, documenting the dual trigger: confirmed US CENTCOM kinetic strikes on Iranian missile sites on 25 May 2026 and the Iranian foreign ministry's public retaliation threat issued on 26 May 2026. Level 5 activation must cascade within 24 hours to vessel operations, cargo scheduling, insurance, and crisis communications functions, each of which must acknowledge receipt and confirm their Level 5 response actions in writing. Completion is evidenced by the signed Level 5 declaration, the timestamped cascade acknowledgements from each function, and a live incident log entry dated no later than 27 May 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic escalation protocol does not specify the dual-trigger evidence standard (CENTCOM strike confirmation plus foreign ministry retaliation threat) or the 24-hour cascade requirement across named functions, which this incident requires given the speed of Iranian signalling following the 26 May denunciation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct a formal force majeure assessment against all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 72 hours of the 25 May 2026 strikes (deadline: 28 May 2026 23:59 UTC), the General Counsel and Head of Commercial must jointly complete a formal force majeure assessment covering all cargo contracts routed through the Gulf of Oman or Strait of Hormuz, with explicit attention to QatarEnergy LNG sale-and-purchase agreements whose FM clauses may be triggered by the confirmed Hormuz closure in effect since late February 2026; the assessment must identify for each contract: (a) whether the confirmed closure constitutes a qualifying FM event under the contract's governing law, (b) the required notice period and addressee, and (c) any cure or mitigation obligations that would defeat an FM invocation. A prioritised counterparty notification schedule, approved by both accountable officers, must be distributed to the commercial team by the 72-hour deadline, with draft FM notices ready for same-day dispatch to the highest-exposure counterparties. Evidence of completion is the signed assessment document, the prioritised notification schedule, and the draft FM notice templates on file.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic FM assessment requirement does not specify the QatarEnergy LNG SPA exposure, the three-part per-contract analysis (qualifying event, notice mechanics, cure obligations), or the draft-notice-ready output standard, all of which are necessary given the confirmed multi-month Hormuz closure and the volume of stranded LNG cargo.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Brief the board by 29 May 2026 on
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 29 May 2026, the Chief Strategy Officer must deliver a board-level briefing paper quantifying geopolitical contagion risk from the confirmed Hormuz closure to fertiliser supply chains, structured around the confirmed 74% collapse in crude and condensate loadings (from 16.6M to 4.3M bpd, Q4 2025 to Q1 2026) as the baseline demand signal, and explicitly addressing the strait's role as a transit corridor for ammonia and urea exports serving agricultural import-dependent markets; the paper must present at least two supply-chain stress scenarios (90-day and 180-day continued closure) with estimated downstream impact on fertiliser availability and pricing, and must identify any company counterparties or off-take agreements exposed to Gulf-origin ammonia or urea supply. The briefing must be formally tabled as a board agenda item, not circulated as supplementary reading, and the board's response actions must be minuted. Evidence of completion is the dated briefing paper, the board meeting agenda confirming its tabled status, and the relevant board minutes.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic board briefing requirement does not anchor the fertiliser contagion analysis to the confirmed 74% crude loading collapse as a parallel demand-signal, nor does it require the specific ammonia/urea counterparty exposure mapping or the two named stress-scenario horizons that the board needs to make actionable decisions.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Re-route all vessels previously scheduled for Gulf of
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: With immediate effect from 26 May 2026, the Head of Fleet Operations must re-route all company-controlled or chartered vessels with Strait of Hormuz or Gulf of Oman transits scheduled in the 30-day window of 26 May–24 June 2026 via Cape of Good Hope or, where cargo type and shipper consent permits, via the Petroline (East-West Pipeline) alternative; simultaneously, a freeze on all new vessel booking commitments into the Hormuz/Gulf of Oman corridor must be enforced until UKMTO issues a formal updated threat status clearance post the reported 26 May tanker explosion in the Gulf of Oman — if that explosion is confirmed, the freeze must be extended and documented as triggered by that event. A re-routing execution log listing each affected vessel, its original scheduled transit date, its assigned alternative routing, and the estimated voyage cost differential must be compiled and submitted to the CFO within 48 hours. Evidence of completion is the signed booking freeze notice, the re-routing execution log, and written CFO acknowledgement of the cost-differential report.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic re-routing directive does not specify the 30-day transit window, the named Cape of Good Hope and Petroline alternatives, the UKMTO clearance condition for lifting the booking freeze, or the cost-differential reporting obligation to the CFO — all of which are operationally necessary given the confirmed 97% transit volume collapse and the reported ongoing explosion risk.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Engage flag-state authorities and P&I clubs within 72
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within 72 hours of the 25 May 2026 strikes (deadline: 28 May 2026 23:59 UTC), the Chief Compliance Officer must initiate formal engagement with the flag-state authority of each company-controlled vessel that has transited or is scheduled to transit the northern Larak Island channel since 25 February 2026, and with the relevant P&I club underwriters, to obtain written legal opinion on whether payment of IRGC-imposed transit tolls denominated in Chinese yuan constitutes a sanctionable transaction under OFAC's Iran-related designations — referencing OFAC's confirmed 39 Iran-linked vessel designations in Q1 2026 as the enforcement baseline. The CCO must maintain a vessel-by-vessel sanctions-exposure register recording: toll payment dates, yuan amounts, payment routing, and the flag-state/P&I legal opinion received or pending for each. Any vessel for which legal opinion cannot be obtained within the 72-hour window must be immediately escalated to external OFAC counsel. Evidence of completion is the vessel-level register, the engagement letters to flag states and P&I clubs, and any external counsel instruction letters.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic sanctions-exposure check does not address the specific IRGC yuan-denominated toll mechanism at Larak Island, the OFAC Q1 2026 enforcement baseline of 39 designations, or the per-vessel register with payment-routing detail that is required to demonstrate OFAC compliance at the transaction level.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review all counterparty relationships with entities transiting the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 30 May 2026, the Chief Compliance Officer must complete a counterparty sanctions-exposure review covering all commercial relationships — charterers, agents, bunker suppliers, port operators, and cargo intermediaries — with entities that have transited the IRGC-controlled northern Larak Island channel since the confirmed Hormuz closure began in late February 2026, cross-referencing each counterparty against OFAC's SDN list and the 39 Iran-linked vessel designations issued in Q1 2026; any counterparty with unresolved exposure must be placed on a commercial hold pending legal clearance, and any existing contract with such a party must be reviewed for termination rights or amendment obligations. The review output must be a tiered counterparty risk register (Clear / Monitor / Hold / Terminate) approved by both the CCO and General Counsel, with hold and terminate decisions actioned in writing to the relevant commercial teams by 30 May 2026. Evidence of completion is the signed tiered register and the written commercial-hold or termination notices where applicable.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic counterparty review requirement does not specify the Larak Island channel as the geographic filter, the OFAC Q1 2026 designation count as the cross-reference benchmark, or the four-tier (Clear/Monitor/Hold/Terminate) output format with dual CCO/GC sign-off that is necessary to demonstrate a defensible OFAC compliance process at this severity level.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Update country-risk ratings for Iran
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Within five business days of the 25 May 2026 strikes (deadline: 1 June 2026), the Head of Country Risk must formally upgrade country-risk ratings for (a) Iran, (b) the Gulf of Oman corridor including Omani territorial waters adjacent to the strait, and (c) UAE-adjacent waters within the Hormuz approach zone to 'Extreme' in the company's country-risk register, citing as the primary trigger the confirmed breakdown of the nominal ceasefire evidenced by Iran's foreign ministry public denunciation on 26 May 2026 and the confirmed Hormuz closure since late February 2026; the upgraded ratings must automatically trigger the downstream controls mapped to 'Extreme' classification in the risk framework (including insurance review, vessel exclusion zone enforcement, and counterparty credit limit reduction). The updated register entries must be circulated to underwriting, treasury, operations, and legal within 24 hours of the rating change. Evidence of completion is the dated register showing the three upgraded entries, the downstream control trigger log, and the distribution receipt confirmation from all four receiving functions.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic country-risk rating update does not name the three specific geographic zones requiring upgrade, does not anchor the trigger to the confirmed foreign ministry denunciation date, and does not specify the downstream control cascade that 'Extreme' classification must automatically activate under the company's own risk framework.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Activate duty-of-care and crew welfare protocols for all
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: With immediate effect from 26 May 2026, the Head of Crew Welfare and the Director of Fleet Operations must jointly activate duty-of-care protocols for all company-controlled or managed vessels confirmed as part of the estimated 2,000-vessel backlog stranded in the Persian Gulf, conducting within 48 hours a vessel-by-vessel audit of: (a) victualling stocks against a 30-day minimum MLC 2006 standard, (b) medical supplies and telemedical access, and (c) crew change overdue status relative to MLC 2006 maximum service period limits; any vessel failing any of the three thresholds must be escalated within 24 hours of audit completion to the relevant flag-state authority and to the ITF with a formal welfare intervention request. A consolidated crew welfare status dashboard, updated daily, must be maintained for all stranded vessels and made available to flag states and P&I clubs on request. Evidence of completion is the 48-hour audit report, the escalation log for non-compliant vessels, and the live dashboard access confirmation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic duty-of-care protocol does not specify the three MLC 2006 audit dimensions (victualling, medical, crew change overdue status), the 48-hour audit deadline against the 2,000-vessel backlog scale, or the mandatory ITF escalation pathway for non-compliant vessels — all of which are required given the reported scale of seafarer stranding in the Persian Gulf.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Test business continuity scenarios in which Hormuz remains
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 8 June 2026 (14 days from the 25 May trigger), the Chief Financial Officer and Head of Business Continuity must complete and present to the Executive Committee a documented business continuity stress test modelling a 90-day Hormuz closure extension from 25 May 2026 through to 22 August 2026, with scenarios built on the confirmed baseline of 97% transit volume collapse and the confirmed 74% crude loading decline (16.6M to 4.3M bpd Q4 2025–Q1 2026); the model must quantify cumulative revenue loss, working capital impact, and charter contract default risk for each additional 30-day increment, and must identify the point at which the company's liquidity reserves are exhausted absent mitigating actions. The stress test must also specify the three highest-priority mitigating actions (e.g., drawdown of revolving credit facility, acceleration of Cape re-routing surcharges, force majeure invocation) with decision triggers for each. Evidence of completion is the signed stress-test report, the ExCo presentation deck with attendance record, and the documented decision-trigger thresholds.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic BCP scenario test does not anchor the 90-day extension to the 25 May 2026 start date, does not require the 30-day incremental loss quantification against the confirmed supply-deficit baseline, and does not mandate identification of liquidity exhaustion thresholds and pre-approved mitigation triggers — all of which are necessary for an actionable rather than illustrative continuity exercise.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Immediately update voyage risk assessments to flag the
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: With immediate effect from 26 May 2026, the Head of Voyage Risk must reissue voyage risk assessments for all vessels operating in or transiting toward the Gulf of Oman to classify the corridor as an active kinetic zone, citing the confirmed US CENTCOM strikes of 25 May 2026 on Iranian missile sites and IRGC boats as the primary trigger; a hard freeze on new vessel commitments into the Gulf of Oman and Strait of Hormuz approaches must be enforced until UKMTO issues a formal updated threat status — if the reported 26 May tanker explosion in the Gulf of Oman is confirmed by UKMTO, the kinetic zone classification must be extended northward to encompass the full northern Arabian Sea approach corridor and the freeze extended accordingly. All vessels currently within the Gulf of Oman must receive an immediate master's advisory recommending anchorage at the nearest safe haven outside the 50-nautical-mile Hormuz approach zone pending UKMTO clearance. Evidence of completion is the reissued voyage risk assessment documents with timestamps, the freeze enforcement notice, and the master's advisory transmission logs.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic voyage risk update does not specify the 50-nautical-mile Hormuz approach zone boundary, the UKMTO clearance condition for lifting the freeze, the conditional northward extension trigger tied to confirmation of the reported tanker explosion, or the immediate master's advisory obligation for vessels already inside the corridor.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Ensure all vessels operating within 50 nautical miles
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: By 28 May 2026 (72 hours from the 25 May strikes), the Head of Marine Insurance must verify, for every company-controlled or managed vessel operating within 50 nautical miles of the Strait of Hormuz as of 25 May 2026, that its war-risk insurance endorsement is: (a) active and not lapsed due to the ceasefire-violation reclassification event, (b) repriced to the current Lloyd's market range of 0.2%–0.4% of hull value per voyage (up from the pre-escalation 0.125% baseline), and (c) free of any clause that voids coverage upon a ceasefire-violation finding — given that Iran's foreign ministry formally declared the 25 May US strikes a ceasefire violation on 26 May 2026; any vessel whose coverage is lapsed, under-priced, or potentially void must be immediately withdrawn from the 50-nautical-mile zone or have coverage reinstated before any further transit. A vessel-level war-risk compliance register, signed by the Head of Marine Insurance and reviewed by the CFO, must be completed and filed by the 28 May deadline. Evidence of completion is the signed vessel-level register, broker confirmation letters for each repriced endorsement, and the withdrawal or reinstatement notices for any non-compliant vessels.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic war-risk insurance control does not specify the ceasefire-violation clause void risk triggered by Iran's 26 May foreign ministry declaration, the specific repricing verification from the named 0.125% baseline to the 0.2%–0.4% current range, or the mandatory withdrawal-or-reinstate consequence for non-compliant vessels — all of which are directly created by the specific legal and market facts of this incident.

incidents.headline: Starbucks Korea 'Tank Day' massacre-linked campaign triggers national boycott
incidents.summary: On May 18, 2026, Starbucks Korea — operated by Shinsegae Group — ran a 'Tank Day' promotion for a large tumbler on the 46th anniversary of the Gwangju pro-democracy massacre, using imagery and a slogan widely read as mocking victims of the brutal 1980 military crackdown. Public outrage was immediate: the Starbucks Korea CEO was fired, police opened an investigation, South Korea's president condemned the campaign on X, the Interior Minister banned Starbucks products from government events, and the Shinsegae Group chairman issued a second public apology at a Seoul press conference on May 26, 2026. A Shinsegae official told Reuters that sales had fallen dramatically, and government-amplified boycott calls spread nationally.

secondary_mappings.why: The campaign caused direct, severe damage to the Starbucks Korea brand identity and required emergency brand management intervention including cancellation, public apology and CEO dismissal.
Government officials including the President and Interior Minister publicly condemned the brand, making government and civic stakeholder relations a primary aggravating dimension.
The campaign is under police investigation for potential intent to mock victims of a historical atrocity, implicating the ethics and conduct framework for employees and management.
Internal review revealed employees refused to hand over smartphones to management, indicating a process-control breakdown in campaign approval and post-incident investigation.

incidents.severity_rationale: National consumer boycott endorsed by the head of state and cabinet ministers, police investigation opened, CEO dismissed, and sales materially down — the highest severity category for brand-trust destruction with government enforcement overlap.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: Sales fallen dramatically (exact figure not disclosed); Starbucks Korea CEO dismissed

Curbing Extreme Mockery and Hatred Requires Civic Common Sense

adaptive_objectives.statement: Maintain and protect the organisational brand and public trust, demonstrated here by ensuring the Shinsegae Group's broader retail and development portfolio is not irrevocably damaged by the Starbucks Korea campaign failure.
Prevent and manage campaign-level reputational crises by ensuring all marketing content is reviewed against South Korean national commemoration calendars before launch.

Restore and rebuild organisational reputation following the May 18 'Tank Day' incident, including the 3 trillion KRW 'Grand Starfield Gwangju' development project credibility with Gwangju civic groups.
Protect and manage stakeholder relationships, specifically with the South Korean government, the Interior Ministry, Gwangju City, and the May 18 civic movement organisations.
Maintain ethical marketing governance and cultural sensitivity standards across all Shinsegae Group brand teams operating in historically sensitive Korean contexts.
Ensure operational processes for campaign approval include mandatory cultural-sensitivity and historical-calendar screening before any promotional launch.
Maintain clear campaign approval authority and accountability structures so that a 'Tank Day'-class failure cannot be approved without executive sign-off from the Starbucks Korea CEO level.
Ensure rapid crisis-response operational procedures exist so that a campaign like 'Tank Day' can be halted within hours, not days, and a public statement issued within 24 hours.
