adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Starbucks Korea must implement a crisis communications holding
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Starbucks Korea must develop and rehearse a crisis communications holding statement protocol — inclusive of pre-approved Korean and English language templates, a designated spokesperson chain of command, and a mandatory 60-minute maximum response clock — calibrated specifically for campaigns with national-commemoration exposure, to address the documented absence of any timely official response in the hours following the May 18, 2026 'Tank Day' launch. The protocol must be stress-tested against a simulated historically-sensitive campaign scenario before September 30, 2026, with documented evidence of the rehearsal provided to the Shinsegae Group board. Accountability for protocol activation rests with the incoming Starbucks Korea CEO, with escalation to the Shinsegae Group Chairman's office if the CEO position remains vacant.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a crisis communications holding protocol; this adaptation specifies the exact failure mode — no timely response on May 18, 2026 — and mandates bilingual templates, a named escalation chain to the post-dismissal successor CEO, and a hard 60-minute clock tied to the documented hours-long gap.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must establish a national-commemoration content-screening gate
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group's Brand Governance Office must implement a standing calendar gate that automatically quarantines for mandatory senior review any campaign copy, product name, promotional slogan, or visual asset submitted for Starbucks Korea, Emart, SSG.com, or Shinsegae Department Store channels if its launch date falls on or within seven calendar days of May 18 (Gwangju Uprising anniversary), June 10 (June Democracy Movement), April 19 (April Revolution), or any date designated a national commemoration day under South Korean law. The gate must flag content containing military vehicle imagery, protest-era visual references, or terminology associated with the 1980 crackdown — the specific failure vectors present in the 'Tank Day' tumbler campaign — and must route flagged submissions to the cultural-sensitivity advisory panel established under AC-REP-009-GJU-01 before any production spend is authorised. Documented gate-check records must be retained for a minimum of three years and made available to the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation on request.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control calls for a national-commemoration content-screening gate; this adaptation names the four specific Korean democracy anniversaries that must be covered, enumerates the exact failure vectors from the May 18 campaign (military imagery, protest-era terminology, tank visuals), and links the gate output to the separately mandated advisory panel.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Starbucks Korea's marketing approval workflow must require a
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Starbucks Korea's marketing approval workflow must be restructured to require a wet-signature or auditable digital sign-off by the sitting CEO — or, during any CEO vacancy such as the one created by the dismissal of Sohn Jeong-hyun following the May 18, 2026 incident, by the Shinsegae Group Chief Marketing Officer — for any campaign copy that references consumer-facing cultural, historical, or commemorative themes, before the campaign is released to production. The sign-off record must explicitly attest that the approving executive has reviewed the campaign against the Korean National Memorial Day calendar gate and that the cultural-sensitivity advisory panel has cleared the submission; absence of either attestation invalidates the sign-off. This requirement must be embedded in Starbucks Korea's campaign management system as a non-bypassable workflow gate by October 31, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires named executive sign-off at CEO level; this adaptation addresses the specific post-dismissal vacancy risk created by the termination of CEO Sohn Jeong-hyun and conditions the sign-off's validity on two upstream checks — the calendar gate and the advisory panel clearance — that were absent for the Tank Day campaign.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must produce a post-incident reputational-loss register
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group's Chief Financial Officer must produce, within 30 days of this control's activation, a formal reputational-loss register that carries at minimum the following distinct financial risk line items, each with a quantified base-case and stress-case exposure: (1) the 427.5 billion KRW Starbucks Korea prepaid-card liability, assessed against the probability and velocity of a consumer refund movement triggered by the ongoing boycott; (2) revenue and relationship disruption from the Interior Minister's ban on Starbucks Korea products at government events; (3) the 3 trillion KRW 'Grand Starfield Gwangju' development project, assessed against community demands for full withdrawal; and (4) inter-affiliate contagion risk across the 213 billion KRW Shinsegae Food supply relationship and the 25 billion KRW Shinsegae I&C IT contract. The register must be presented to the Shinsegae Group board and to GIC as the 32.5% minority shareholder, with quarterly refresh obligations until the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation is closed.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a post-incident reputational-loss register; this adaptation converts the three named financial exposures from the incident facts into four discrete, quantified line items and adds the GIC minority-shareholder briefing obligation and a sunset condition tied to the police investigation.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must immediately brief its board of
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group's Chairman must convene an emergency board-level briefing within five business days of this control's activation to assess reputational contagion risk across all affiliate brands — specifically Emart (which holds 67.5% of Starbucks Korea and is the entity named in the franchise relationship with Starbucks Corp.), SSG.com, Shinsegae Department Store, Shinsegae Food, and Shinsegae I&C — arising from the Starbucks Korea 'Tank Day' incident and the Democratic Party's internal guidelines banning Starbucks use for party events. The briefing package must include a brand-association heat map showing consumer perception linkage between each affiliate and Starbucks Korea, current sales trend data disaggregated by affiliate, and a scenario analysis covering escalation if the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation produces charges against Chairman Chung Yong-jin or former CEO Sohn Jeong-hyun. The board must record a formal resolution acknowledging receipt of the briefing and directing specific containment actions for each high-exposure affiliate.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a board briefing on affiliate reputational exposure; this adaptation names the specific Shinsegae affiliates at risk, grounds the contagion analysis in the Democratic Party ban and the police investigation as live amplifiers, and requires a formal board resolution — not merely a presentation — with affiliate-specific containment directives.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Starbucks Corp
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Starbucks Corp. (Seattle) must, within 15 business days of this control's activation, formally notify Emart — as the 67.5% majority operator of Starbucks Korea — that it is conducting a material-breach assessment under the brand-standards and termination provisions of the Korea franchise and licensing agreement, specifically evaluating whether the 'Tank Day' campaign of May 18, 2026 — which used imagery and slogans publicly interpreted as mocking victims of the Gwangju Uprising on its 46th anniversary, resulting in the dismissal of CEO Sohn Jeong-hyun, a presidential public condemnation, a government gift-ban, and a police investigation against the Chairman — constitutes a breach of brand integrity, reputational, or cultural-sensitivity obligations under those terms. Starbucks Corp. must share the assessment findings with Emart and with GIC as the minority shareholder within 45 days of initiation, and must document what remediation commitments, if any, are required as a condition of continued licensure.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires invocation of franchise agreement review clauses; this adaptation names the specific contracting parties (Starbucks Corp. and Emart as the 67.5% operator), cites the five concrete post-incident consequences that constitute the materiality threshold, and includes GIC as a notifiable party given its disclosed equity stake.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must appoint an independent cultural-sensitivity advisory
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group must, within 60 days of the May 26, 2026 press conference at which Chairman Chung Yong-jin pledged an internal investigation, formally constitute an independent Cultural Sensitivity Advisory Panel with a minimum composition of: one representative nominated by the May 18 Memorial Foundation (Gwangju); one independent Korean historian specialising in the 1980 Gwangju Uprising and the broader democratisation movement; one senior communications professional with demonstrable crisis and cultural-risk experience in the South Korean market; and one Starbucks Korea employee representative. The panel must be granted a standing mandate to review all major seasonal campaign briefs for Starbucks Korea — and, where Shinsegae Group determines contagion risk is material, for Emart and Shinsegae Department Store — before external production is authorised, with a documented panel clearance record required as a condition of the CEO-level sign-off gate under AC-REP-011-GJU-01. Panel terms of reference, composition, and first review schedule must be publicly disclosed as part of the internal investigation outcome that Chairman Chung pledged at the May 26 press conference.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires an independent cultural-sensitivity panel including a May 18 Memorial Foundation representative; this adaptation specifies the full minimum panel composition, creates a hard linkage to the CEO sign-off gate, and anchors the disclosure obligation to the specific public commitment made by Chairman Chung at the May 26 press conference.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Starbucks Korea must publish a transparent public account
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Consistent with Chairman Chung Yong-jin's public pledge at the May 26, 2026 Seoul press conference to disclose the results of an internal investigation, Starbucks Korea — with oversight from the Shinsegae Group board — must publish a transparent public account of the 'Tank Day' campaign approval process no later than August 31, 2026, naming: (a) each internal approval stage through which the campaign passed, from creative brief to final sign-off; (b) the role title (without personal data where legally constrained) at each stage that approved the copy; (c) the specific stage at which the May 18 date significance and Gwangju Uprising association were, or were not, assessed; and (d) whether the outgoing CEO Sohn Jeong-hyun's documented sign-off, or the absence thereof, was a factor in the campaign's progression to launch. The published account must be made available in Korean and English, shared with the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation, and referenced in Shinsegae Group's next investor disclosure.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a transparent public account of the approval process; this adaptation specifies the four mandatory disclosure elements derived from the incident's documented failure points, sets the August 31 deadline aligned with the police investigation, and requires dual-language publication consistent with the international franchise and investor context.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must produce a stakeholder-engagement plan specifically
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group must appoint a dedicated Gwangju Stakeholder Engagement Lead — at minimum a Vice President-level representative with authority to make binding commitments — and deliver a formal stakeholder-engagement plan to Gwangju City civic groups and May 18 Democratization Movement organisations within 45 days of this control's activation, addressing explicitly: (a) the status of the 3 trillion KRW 'Grand Starfield Gwangju' development project in light of community demands for full withdrawal; (b) Shinsegae Group's concrete commitments to the May 18 Memorial Foundation, including any proposed financial contributions, permanent campaign restrictions, or structural governance changes; and (c) a proposed ongoing dialogue mechanism — meeting frequency, escalation route, and escalation authority — through which civic groups can raise concerns before, not after, brand decisions affecting Gwangju are taken. Progress against the engagement plan must be reported to the Shinsegae Group board quarterly and disclosed publicly on an annual basis until the Gwangju development project reaches a documented resolution.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a stakeholder-engagement plan for Gwangju groups; this adaptation ties the plan directly to the 3 trillion KRW development project as the specific leverage point, mandates a named VP-level lead with binding authority, and establishes a forward-looking dialogue mechanism to address the structural absence of civic consultation that preceded the May 18 launch.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group's HR and marketing functions must conduct
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group's HR and Marketing functions must jointly design and deliver a mandatory Korean historical-commemoration and cultural-sensitivity training programme — covering specifically the Gwangju Uprising (May 18, 1980), the June Democracy Movement, the April Revolution, and the contemporary social significance of state violence imagery in South Korean public discourse — to all brand managers, campaign producers, and marketing approval-chain personnel across Starbucks Korea, Emart, Shinsegae Department Store, and Shinsegae Food, with 100% completion required before April 18, 2027 (30 days prior to the next May 18 anniversary). The programme must include a module specifically examining the 'Tank Day' campaign as an anonymised case study of how military-era imagery was approved without historical sensitivity review; completion records must be maintained per employee, and non-completion must block access to the campaign approval system's submit function. The training design must be reviewed and endorsed by the Cultural Sensitivity Advisory Panel constituted under AC-REP-009-GJU-01.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a cultural-sensitivity and Korean historical-commemoration training programme; this adaptation names the four specific anniversaries and the exact failure mechanism to be covered in case-study form, creates a hard technical block (system access restriction) for non-completion, and ties the curriculum endorsement to the separately mandated advisory panel.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must establish a whistleblower or escalation
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group must establish, by September 30, 2026, a confidential campaign-content escalation channel — accessible to all Starbucks Korea, Emart, and Shinsegae Group brand and marketing staff — through which employees can flag historically or culturally sensitive content concerns to a designated Senior Reviewer who sits outside the brand's direct marketing reporting line, without risk of managerial retaliation. The channel must be operable via a minimum of two submission modes (e.g. named email alias to Group Legal and an anonymous web form), must guarantee written acknowledgement of each submission within 24 hours, and must require the Senior Reviewer to document a reasoned disposition of each flagged concern before the relevant campaign proceeds to the CEO sign-off stage under AC-REP-011-GJU-01. The existence, submission volume, and disposition rate of the channel must be reported to the Shinsegae Group board annually, and the channel's design must be disclosed to the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation as evidence of remediation, given that the absence of any effective internal dissent mechanism allowed the May 18, 2026 'Tank Day' campaign to proceed to launch.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a whistleblower/escalation channel for culturally sensitive content; this adaptation addresses the specific failure mode — no effective internal dissent reached or stopped the Tank Day campaign — by mandating dual submission modes, a 24-hour acknowledgement SLA, a documented disposition before the sign-off gate, and board-level annual reporting as a structural accountability layer.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Shinsegae Group must redesign Starbucks Korea's campaign approval
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Shinsegae Group's Brand Governance Office must redesign the Starbucks Korea campaign approval workflow — within the campaign management system used for all consumer-facing promotional copy — to embed a non-bypassable Korean National Memorial Day and democratisation-anniversary calendar check as a mandatory gate between the creative-brief approval stage and the production-spend authorisation stage, documented by a system-generated timestamp and the approving reviewer's identity. The gate must cross-reference campaign launch dates against a maintained calendar covering at minimum: May 18 (Gwangju Uprising), June 10 (June Democracy Movement), April 19 (April Revolution), August 15 (Liberation Day), and any additional dates designated by the Ministry of the Interior and Safety — the same ministry whose head, Yoon Ho-jung, imposed the Starbucks Korea government-event ban — as national commemoration days. The redesigned workflow must be live and tested before October 31, 2026, with evidence of gate functionality (including a test record showing a simulated May 18-dated campaign being correctly quarantined) provided to the Shinsegae Group board and retained for the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a campaign approval workflow with a mandatory Korean National Memorial Day calendar check-gate; this adaptation names the specific calendar dates that must be encoded, ties the gate's authority to the Interior Ministry (whose Minister imposed the government-event ban as a direct consequence of the May 18 failure), and requires a documented test record as proof of function.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Starbucks Korea must produce a documented post-incident root-cause
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Starbucks Korea, under the oversight of the Shinsegae Group board and consistent with Chairman Chung Yong-jin's public commitment at the May 26, 2026 press conference, must complete and publish a documented post-incident root-cause analysis of the 'Tank Day' campaign by August 31, 2026, covering: (a) the sequence of approval stages through which the campaign passed from initial brief to May 18, 2026 launch; (b) identification by role title of the personnel who held approval authority at each stage, and whether they exercised it; (c) the specific process control — calendar check, cultural review, executive sign-off — that was absent, bypassed, or failed; and (d) a determination of whether the dismissal of CEO Sohn Jeong-hyun addresses the root cause or whether systemic workflow or governance failures remain unresolved. The root-cause analysis must be shared with the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation, provided to Starbucks Corp. (Seattle) in connection with the franchise assessment under AC-REP-007-GJU-01, and used as the primary input document for the workflow redesign mandated under AC-OPS-001-GJU-01.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a documented post-incident root-cause analysis as promised at the May 26 press conference; this adaptation specifies the four mandatory analytical elements, requires the RCA to address whether the CEO dismissal resolves the root cause or leaves systemic failures open, and creates explicit output linkages to the police investigation, the franchise assessment, and the workflow redesign controls.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Starbucks Korea must implement an emergency campaign-takedown procedure
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Starbucks Korea must design, document, and test an emergency campaign-takedown procedure — covering all consumer-facing channels including the Starbucks Korea mobile application, the official website, in-store point-of-sale materials, and social media accounts — with a maximum two-hour execution window measured from the moment a designated Crisis Response Owner issues a takedown instruction, reflecting the documented failure to achieve rapid removal of the 'Tank Day' materials following the public outcry that began on May 18, 2026. The procedure must include: a named Crisis Response Owner role (assigned to the incoming CEO or a designated deputy during any vacancy); a channel-by-channel takedown runbook with responsible system owners and access credentials management confirmed in advance; and a communication template to be published simultaneously with takedown confirming the campaign has been withdrawn. A full simulation exercise — using a scenario based on a fictitious but structurally analogous May 18-dated campaign — must be completed and documented before September 30, 2026, with results presented to the Shinsegae Group board and retained for the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency investigation.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a tested emergency campaign-takedown procedure with a two-hour window; this adaptation specifies all four channel types that must be covered (app, web, in-store, social), addresses the CEO-vacancy risk created by the dismissal of Sohn Jeong-hyun by naming a deputy-assignment requirement, and grounds the simulation deadline in the specific May 18, 2026 failure to remove materials promptly.

incidents.headline: Standard Chartered CEO apologizes for 'lower value human capital' AI remarks
incidents.summary: Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters sparked a global reputational crisis after describing employees whose jobs could be automated as 'lower value human capital' at an investor event in Hong Kong around May 20, 2026, coinciding with an announcement of up to 8,000 job cuts. The phrase drew immediate condemnation on social media, a public rebuke from former Singaporean President Halimah Yacob, and criticism from JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon who called the comments 'inartful.' On May 26, 2026, Winters issued a public apology via LinkedIn, acknowledging the remarks had 'caused upset to some colleagues,' but his follow-up LinkedIn post doubling down drew hundreds of further negative reactions. The episode amplified anxieties around AI-driven job displacement and dehumanising corporate language globally.

secondary_mappings.why: The remarks triggered public condemnation from a former head of state and a peer CEO, damaging investor, employee, and public stakeholder confidence simultaneously.
The comments directly concern an 8,000-role reduction linked to AI, creating acute employee relations damage and anxiety about job security across the bank's 82,000-strong workforce.
The CEO's two-step communications response — internal memo followed by public LinkedIn apology — constitutes active crisis communications management that failed to stem backlash.
The episode exposed a broader strategic communications gap in Standard Chartered's AI transformation narrative, damaging the credibility of its workforce restructuring strategy.

incidents.severity_rationale: Significant reputational and employee-trust damage for a major global bank, amplified by peer CEO commentary and a former head of state rebuke, but no confirmed financial market impact or regulatory action as of the disclosure date.

sources.title: Standard Chartered CEO apologizes for calling some workers 'lower value human capital' in AI push
CEO of Standard Chartered: I'm sorry for 'lower value human capital' comments
Standard Chartered CEO's 'lower-value human capital' comments draw ire

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure executive public communications — including at investor events and on LinkedIn — are governed by a pre-clearance protocol that prevents language causing foreseeable reputational harm to Standard Chartered and its employees.

Maintain stakeholder trust across Standard Chartered's Asia markets, particularly Singapore and Hong Kong, which generate the bulk of group profits and where the Winters episode has activated direct political scrutiny.

Ensure Standard Chartered's AI-driven workforce transformation programme — specifically the 8,000-role back-office reduction over four years — is communicated and executed in a manner that respects employee dignity and satisfies multi-jurisdictional labour-law obligations.

Provide reskilling and redeployment pathways for employees whose roles are identified as automatable under Standard Chartered's AI-transformation strategy, consistent with the commitments Winters made in his internal memo of approximately May 21, 2026.

Manage the ongoing LinkedIn crisis cycle triggered by Winters' follow-up post to prevent further escalation and restore internal confidence, particularly across Standard Chartered's 85,000-person workforce in Asia.

Ensure Standard Chartered's strategic AI-workforce transformation decisions — including the 15% back-office reduction target — are governed by a board-level framework that accounts for reputational, regulatory, and human-capital risks before public disclosure.

graded_claims.claim: Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters used the phrase 'lower value human capital' at an investor event in Hong Kong on approximately May 20, 2026.
Standard Chartered disclosed plans to eliminate close to 8,000 support roles over the next four years.
Winters issued a public apology via LinkedIn on May 26, 2026, stating the remarks 'caused upset to some colleagues.'
Former Singaporean President Halimah Yacob condemned Winters' terminology in a Facebook post, calling it 'disturbing.'
JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon called Winters' comments 'inartful.'
Winters' follow-up LinkedIn post doubling down on the back-office reduction plan drew hundreds of further negative reactions.
Winters confirmed back-office corporate function roles will be reduced by approximately 15% in the next four years.
Standard Chartered is one of the first global banks to publicly set out how it expects AI to trim headcount.
Winters sent an internal memo to staff on approximately May 21, 2026, seen by Bloomberg News, seeking to reassure employees.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must implement a CEO and Senior
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group General Counsel, Chief People Officer, and Group Head of Communications must jointly establish a CEO and Senior Manager Pre-Clearance Protocol for AI-workforce language within 14 days of this enrichment date, requiring written tri-party sign-off on any script, slide deck, or social media post in which Bill Winters or any Group Executive Committee member addresses headcount reduction, automation, or employee value at investor events, regulatory briefings, or public platforms including LinkedIn. The protocol must include a mandatory terminology review checklist that flags dehumanising or legally sensitive phrasing — anchored explicitly to the failure mode of the May 20, 2026 Hong Kong investor event, where the phrase 'lower value human capital' was delivered without HR or communications review — and must apply retroactively to all scheduled investor communications through December 2026. Completion evidence is a signed protocol document lodged with the Group Board Risk Committee, plus a confirmed pre-clearance log entry for the next Winters public appearance.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a speech pre-clearance protocol; this adaptation names the three accountable sign-off roles, anchors the trigger to the specific May 20 Hong Kong failure, mandates retroactive coverage of the current investor calendar, and requires a logged pre-clearance record as the verifiable completion artefact.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must publish a structured public correction
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Communications must, within 21 days of this enrichment date, publish a structured public correction — distinct from and superseding Winters' May 26, 2026 LinkedIn apology — that explicitly retracts the phrase 'lower value human capital,' names it as inappropriate and inconsistent with Standard Chartered's stated values, and sets out a costed reskilling commitment with at least one named programme, a defined number of eligible roles, and a completion milestone no later than Q4 2027. The correction must be published simultaneously on Standard Chartered's corporate newsroom, LinkedIn, and distributed via press release to Asia-Pacific and UK wire services, and must be reviewed and approved by the Group Board before publication. Evidence of completion is the published correction with a confirmed timestamp, the press release distribution list, and a board approval minute.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a structured public correction; this adaptation specifies the exact retraction wording required, mandates simultaneous multi-channel publication including wire distribution targeting the same outlets that amplified the original remarks, and requires board approval as a governance checkpoint to close the LinkedIn crisis loop created by the follow-up post that drew hundreds of further negative reactions.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered's Group Communications must map all stakeholder
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Communications must, within 72 hours of this enrichment date, produce and present to the Group CEO and Group Chief People Officer a stakeholder response matrix that maps at minimum the following cohorts to tailored, channel-specific response actions: (a) Monetary Authority of Singapore — direct regulatory briefing by the Singapore Country CEO; (b) former heads of state and regional political figures, including former Singaporean President Halimah Yacob whose Facebook rebuke has been reported publicly; (c) institutional investors including Temasek Holdings and other Singaporean sovereign funds with material Standard Chartered exposure; (d) the bank's approximately 85,000 employees globally, segmented by the approximately 8,000 roles designated for reduction. Each cohort entry must name the accountable executive, the communication channel, the message framework, and the response deadline. Completion evidence is the matrix document with a confirmed delivery timestamp and a sign-off record from the Group CEO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires stakeholder mapping; this adaptation names the four specific cohorts that have been activated by this incident, assigns a Singapore Country CEO accountability for the MAS channel, and requires cohort-level granularity down to the 8,000 designated-reduction employees — specificity absent from any generic communications playbook.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must monitor sentiment specifically in Singapore
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Communications must, within five business days of this enrichment date, activate a dedicated sentiment monitoring workstream covering Singapore and Hong Kong social media platforms, political commentary channels, and regional financial press, configured to track keywords including 'lower value human capital,' 'Bill Winters,' 'Standard Chartered layoffs,' and 'Halimah Yacob,' with alert thresholds set to escalate any material spike in negative sentiment to the Group Head of Communications within two hours. A weekly sentiment briefing — covering volume, tone, key influencers, and recommended responses — must be presented to the Group Board Risk Committee at each of its scheduled meetings through at least December 31, 2026, given that former Singaporean President Halimah Yacob's reported Facebook rebuke has created an active and ongoing reputational threat in the MAS jurisdiction. Completion evidence is the activated monitoring dashboard configuration, the first weekly briefing pack, and a confirmed board agenda slot.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires sentiment monitoring; this adaptation names the specific Singapore and Hong Kong channels activated by the Halimah Yacob rebuke, specifies the exact keyword configuration required, and mandates board-level escalation cadence proportionate to the political sensitivity of criticism originating from a former head of state.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must produce a jurisdiction-disaggregated workforce-impact assessment
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Legal and Group People functions must, within 45 days of this enrichment date, produce a jurisdiction-disaggregated workforce-impact assessment for the planned elimination of close to 8,000 support roles over four years, covering at minimum: (a) Singapore — MAS Fair Dealing Guidelines and MAS Notice 634 applicability to affected customer-facing support functions, and whether the reduction triggers mandatory consultation obligations under the Employment Act; (b) United Kingdom — UK Employment Rights Act 1996 thresholds for collective redundancy consultation (20 or more redundancies at one establishment within 90 days), with entity-level headcount data mapped to each Standard Chartered UK legal entity; (c) European Union jurisdictions — Collective Redundancies Directive 98/59/EC minimum consultation periods and notification obligations to national authorities for each EU entity in scope. The assessment must be approved by the Group General Counsel and presented to the Board People and Remuneration Committee before any further public disclosure of reduction timelines. Completion evidence is the signed assessment document and the board committee presentation record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a jurisdiction-disaggregated impact assessment; this adaptation names the three specific legal frameworks — MAS Fair Dealing, UK Employment Rights Act, and EU Collective Redundancies Directive — and requires entity-level headcount mapping, transforming a compliance checklist requirement into an executable legal analysis deliverable tied to the confirmed 8,000-role figure.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must translate Winters' internal memo promise
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief People Officer must, within 90 days of this enrichment date, present to the Group Board for approval a formal Reskilling and Redeployment Programme converting the reskilling and redeployment commitment referenced in Winters' internal memo of approximately May 21, 2026 into a board-ratified programme document that specifies: (a) the total allocated budget in USD; (b) the categories of back-office and support roles eligible for redeployment, cross-referenced against the confirmed 15% back-office reduction target; (c) at least three named reskilling pathways with defined completion timelines no later than Q4 2028; and (d) the disclosure package to be submitted to both the Monetary Authority of Singapore and the UK Financial Conduct Authority within 30 days of board approval. Completion evidence is the board-approved programme document, the MAS submission receipt, and the FCA submission receipt.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a board-approved reskilling programme; this adaptation anchors the obligation directly to the internal memo cited in confirmed/reported facts, requires the programme to cross-reference the confirmed 15% back-office reduction figure, and mandates dual regulatory disclosure to both MAS and the FCA — closing the gap between Winters' memo promise and any verifiable institutional commitment.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must conduct an AI-impact assessment specifically
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief Data and Automation Officer, in coordination with the Chief People Officer, must within 60 days of this enrichment date complete and submit to the Group Board an AI-Impact Assessment for the back-office corporate function roles subject to the confirmed approximately 15% reduction over four years, documenting for each role category: (a) which specific tasks are proposed for AI automation versus human redeployment; (b) the AI tools or platforms driving the displacement; (c) the selection methodology used to identify roles for reduction, including any model-based scoring; and (d) a fairness analysis satisfying MAS FEAT Principle 3 (Fairness), addressing whether the methodology produces disproportionate impact on any demographic group across Standard Chartered's Singapore, Hong Kong, and UK entities. The assessment must be reviewed by an independent internal audit team before board submission. Completion evidence is the completed assessment, the internal audit sign-off memorandum, and the board submission record.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires an AI-impact assessment; this adaptation specifies the MAS FEAT Principle 3 fairness standard as the governing framework, requires task-level granularity mapped to the confirmed 15% back-office reduction, and mandates independent internal audit review — capturing the regulatory accountability dimension that the generic control leaves abstract.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must establish a confidential employee feedback
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Chief People Officer must, by June 30, 2026, establish and make operational a confidential employee feedback channel — accessible to all approximately 8,000 back-office and support staff designated for role reduction — that is independent of line management reporting structures, staffed by a named HR Business Partner team, and capable of receiving written, telephone, and digital submissions in the primary languages of Standard Chartered's Singapore, Hong Kong, India, and UK workforce cohorts. The channel must have a guaranteed response time of no more than five business days per submission, and a monthly summary report — anonymised and aggregated — must be presented to the Board People and Remuneration Committee, directly responding to the colleague upset acknowledged in Winters' May 26, 2026 LinkedIn apology and the negative employee sentiment evidenced by the hundreds of adverse reactions to his follow-up LinkedIn post. Completion evidence is the operational channel with a confirmed go-live date, the multilingual submission interface, and the first monthly summary board report.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a confidential feedback channel; this adaptation names the four primary workforce jurisdictions, mandates multilingual access, sets a five-business-day response SLA, and ties the board reporting cycle directly to the confirmed colleague upset acknowledged in Winters' own LinkedIn apology — grounding the control in an evidentiary fact rather than a generic employee-relations principle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must execute a media rebuttal strategy
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Communications must, within 14 days of this enrichment date, execute a targeted media rebuttal strategy directed at Asia-Pacific outlets — including Singaporean national press, Hong Kong financial media, and the social media channels through which former Singaporean President Halimah Yacob's reported Facebook rebuke was amplified — centred on at least three named, consent-verified case studies of Standard Chartered employees who have been reskilled or redeployed into new roles as a result of automation programmes, each case study citing the employee's original role category, the reskilling pathway undertaken, and the new role. The strategy must be executed by the Group Head of Communications, cleared by legal and HR under the pre-clearance protocol established per AC-REP-019-RIF-01, and must explicitly avoid any language consistent with the 'lower value human capital' framing. Completion evidence is the confirmed publication of case studies in at least three named Asia-Pacific outlets and a media monitoring report showing pick-up within 14 days.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a media rebuttal strategy; this adaptation names the specific Asia-Pacific channel set activated by the Halimah Yacob rebuke, mandates consent-verified employee case studies rather than generic corporate messaging, and requires legal and HR pre-clearance to prevent recurrence of the exact communications failure that created the reputational crisis.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must brief the FCA under SMCR
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group General Counsel must, within 30 days of this enrichment date, prepare and submit a proactive briefing to the UK Financial Conduct Authority under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime, notifying the FCA that Bill Winters — an FCA-approved person holding the Chief Executive SMF1 function — made public statements at the May 20, 2026 Hong Kong investor event that generated material reputational harm to Standard Chartered and required a public apology on May 26, 2026, and setting out the governance remediation steps taken including the pre-clearance protocol, the structured public correction, and the board post-incident review. The briefing must be reviewed by external FCA-specialist counsel before submission and must not characterise the May 26 LinkedIn apology alone as sufficient remediation. Completion evidence is the FCA submission receipt, the external counsel review memorandum, and a log entry in Standard Chartered's SMCR governance register.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires FCA briefing under SMCR; this adaptation names the specific SMF function held by Winters, anchors the notification obligation to the confirmed May 20 investor event and May 26 apology, and requires external specialist counsel review — preventing Standard Chartered from self-certifying remediation adequacy in a senior manager accountability context.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered's board must conduct a post-incident review
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Board must, before its next scheduled board meeting following this enrichment date, conduct a formal post-incident review of the AI-workforce strategy disclosure process that produced the May 20, 2026 Hong Kong investor event failure, specifically examining: (a) the decision-making chain that resulted in the announcement of close to 8,000 job cuts at an external investor event before a coordinated internal employee communication was in place, given that the internal memo to staff was sent approximately May 21, one day after the investor event; (b) whether the AI-reduction plan and associated communications were reviewed by the Board People and Remuneration Committee prior to the investor event; and (c) the adequacy of the existing communications governance framework for AI-workforce disclosures. The review must produce a written findings report with named remediation actions, owners, and deadlines, approved by the full Board and provided to the FCA as part of the SMCR briefing required under AC-REP-011-RIF-01. Completion evidence is the signed board findings report and its inclusion in the FCA briefing package.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a board post-incident review; this adaptation names the specific sequencing failure — investor disclosure preceding employee notification by approximately one day — and requires the review to examine Board People and Remuneration Committee pre-approval gaps, anchoring the governance inquiry in the confirmed timeline rather than a generic process audit.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Standard Chartered must stress-test the 15% back-office AI-reduction
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Standard Chartered's Group Chief Financial Officer and Group Chief Risk Officer must, at least 30 days before the next scheduled investor update at which the AI-reduction programme will be discussed, deliver to the Group Board a stress-test analysis of the confirmed approximately 15% back-office role reduction plan modelling two regulatory intervention scenarios: (a) MAS imposes enhanced workforce consultation requirements in Singapore — including mandatory minimum consultation periods and independent impact assessments — and the cost, timeline extension, and headcount deferral impact on the four-year programme; and (b) the UK Financial Conduct Authority, exercising SMCR oversight following the communications failure, requires Standard Chartered to pause UK-entity role reductions pending governance remediation — modelling the cost of delay and any revised completion date beyond the current four-year horizon. Each scenario must include a revised programme cost estimate, a revised timeline, and a named Board-approved contingency response. Completion evidence is the stress-test report approved by the Board Risk Committee and a confirmed agenda slot at the pre-investor-update board meeting.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires stress-testing of the reduction plan against regulatory intervention; this adaptation names MAS and the FCA as the two specific regulatory actors with plausible intervention rationales arising from this incident, anchors the modelling to the confirmed 15% back-office reduction figure, and ties the delivery deadline to the next investor update — closing the loop between regulatory risk and public disclosure timing.

incidents.headline: Ferrari Luce EV debut triggers brand identity backlash and stock decline
incidents.summary: Ferrari unveiled the Luce, its first all-electric vehicle, in Rome on May 26, 2026 — a five-seater priced at approximately $640,000 designed by Jony Ive's firm LoveFrom — and immediately faced a wave of mockery and criticism described as the most intense product rejection since the Cybertruck. Italian Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini publicly criticised the car's design and cost, while former Ferrari chairman Luca di Montezemolo suggested the company remove the prancing horse logo from the vehicle. Ferrari's stock price fell on the day of the launch, with widespread negative sentiment across news outlets and social media questioning the car's alignment with Ferrari's core brand identity.

secondary_mappings.why: The Luce's launch generated widespread viral mockery and negative social media sentiment spanning the full spectrum from casual critics to industry insiders, amplifying brand damage beyond traditional press.

The backlash directly challenges Ferrari's strategic decision to enter the EV segment with a five-seater design targeting non-traditional buyers, calling into question the commercial viability of the product pivot.
Ferrari's stock price declined on launch day, indicating market-level financial impact from the reputational event that warrants financial risk monitoring.

incidents.severity_rationale: Significant brand identity challenge and stock decline for a top-tier luxury marque, but no confirmed boycott, regulatory action, or material financial loss disclosed; senior political criticism and viral rejection are real but contained to the product launch context.
incidents.financial_impact_disclosed: Stock price decline on May 26, 2026 (exact quantum not disclosed)

sources.title: Ferrari's first EV is not for you

adaptive_objectives.statement: Protect Ferrari's brand equity and prancing horse identity perception among UHNW customers and investors following the Luce launch backlash and Montezemolo's public logo-removal suggestion
Manage and contain the reputational damage arising from the Luce's design departure from Ferrari's heritage, including criticism from Italian government officials and former leadership
Monitor and respond to the social media and earned-media sentiment cycle triggered by the Luce launch, which analysts characterised as the sharpest design-driven stock reaction ever seen

Assess whether Ferrari's EV product strategy — including the 20% EV lineup target for 2030, the $230M Maranello factory, and the Luce positioning — remains defensible in light of the launch-day market verdict
Quantify the financial exposure from the Luce launch to Ferrari's market capitalisation, the $230M EV factory asset base, and projected Q4 2026 delivery revenues

graded_claims.claim: Ferrari unveiled the Luce, its first all-electric vehicle, in Rome on May 26, 2026
The Luce is a five-seater priced at approximately $640,000 (EUR 550,000)
The Luce was designed by Jony Ive's firm LoveFrom
Ferrari's Milan-listed shares fell approximately 6-8% on the day of the launch
Italian Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini publicly criticised the car's design and cost on X
Former Ferrari chairman Luca di Montezemolo suggested the company remove the prancing horse logo from the vehicle
Ferrari's stock is down more than 32% over the last 12 months
Analyst Anthony Dick of Oddo BHF called it the sharpest stock reaction ever seen for a car design
Equita analysts wrote that the Luce will not generate significant volumes capable of bolstering Ferrari's earnings
Ferrari scaled back its electric ambitions at end of 2025, projecting EVs at 20% of lineup in 2030, down from a prior 40% target
Ferrari allegedly delayed a second EV model due to demand concerns
The backlash was described as the most intense product rejection since the Cybertruck
Ferrari invested $230 million in a new EV factory in Maranello, which opened in 2024
Deliveries of the Luce are scheduled to begin in Q4 2026

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Ferrari must activate a named executive spokesperson protocol
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Chief Communications Officer must designate, by 18:00 CET on May 27, 2026, a named senior executive — distinct from CEO Benedetto Vigna, who has already spoken on launch day — to serve as the authorised spokesperson for all responses to Matteo Salvini's X post and Luca di Montezemolo's logo-removal suggestion, with a prepared holding statement reviewed by Ferrari's legal and public affairs teams that acknowledges the criticism without characterising either figure as politically adversarial. The spokesperson protocol must specify approved channels (Italian financial press, Corriere della Sera, Il Sole 24 Ore), prohibited channels (direct social-media engagement with Salvini's X thread), and escalation criteria if either figure makes a second public statement before May 30. Evidence of completion: a signed spokesperson designation memo, the approved holding statement, and a channel matrix filed with the CCO by 18:00 CET May 27.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic master control requires a spokesperson protocol; this adaptation pins the trigger to two named individuals' May 26 statements, specifies Italian media channels appropriate to the political sensitivity of a sitting Deputy Prime Minister, and adds a hard prohibition on direct X engagement to prevent political escalation unique to this incident.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Conduct an immediate brand-identity impact assessment scoped specifically
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Brand and Legal teams must jointly commission, by June 2, 2026, a trademark-equity audit specifically scoped to the Prancing Horse (Cavallino Rampante) logo's association with the Luce model, triggered by former chairman Luca di Montezemolo's public suggestion on May 26, 2026, that the logo be removed from the vehicle; the audit must quantify (a) the licensing and contractual implications of any logo dissociation from a production Ferrari model, (b) consumer-panel sentiment on whether the Prancing Horse's attachment to the Luce enhances or damages the trademark's luxury-sport equity among existing Ferrari clients and prospective Luce buyers in Italy, the US, and the Gulf, and (c) precedent risk — whether a public chairman-level suggestion of logo removal, if unaddressed, becomes the dominant analyst and media frame ahead of Q2 2026 earnings. The audit lead must present findings to the Chief Brand Officer and General Counsel by June 16, 2026, with a written recommendation on logo positioning for the Luce's Q4 delivery communications. Evidence of completion: audit commission letter dated by June 2, panel fieldwork proof-of-launch, and the June 16 findings memo.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a brand-identity impact assessment; this adaptation converts it into a trademark-equity audit with a named trigger event (Montezemolo's May 26 statement), three specifically scoped deliverables tied to the Luce's commercial and legal reality, and a deadline anchored to the Q2 2026 earnings calendar.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Commission a segmented stakeholder perception study within 30
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Investor Relations and Customer Intelligence teams must, within 30 days of the May 26 Luce launch (by June 25, 2026), commission a segmented perception study using separate methodologies for three distinct cohorts: (1) existing Ferrari UHNW clients (qualitative in-depth interviews, n≥30, conducted via Ferrari's regional client relations teams in Italy, the US, and the Gulf), (2) prospective Luce buyers who are not existing Ferrari clients — the cohort underpinning CCO Enrico Galliera's publicly stated thesis that 50% of Luce orders will come from new clients — assessed via an invitation-only digital survey distributed through Ferrari's waiting-list registration system, and (3) institutional investors and sell-side analysts covering Ferrari on Borsa Italiana, assessed via the IR team's existing analyst-relations programme. The study must explicitly test whether the launch-day negative sentiment undermines the '50% new-client' thesis and must produce a risk-quantified finding that can be incorporated into Q2 2026 investor-relations guidance. Evidence of completion: signed study commission by June 2, fieldwork completion certificates, and a consolidated findings deck delivered to the CCO and CFO by June 25.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a stakeholder perception study; this adaptation makes it executable by specifying three named cohorts with distinct methodologies, anchoring the 'new-client' cohort directly to Galliera's publicly staked 50% thesis that the launch-day reaction has now put at risk, and tying the output to Q2 earnings guidance rather than leaving the purpose generic.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Establish a 90-day social-media and earned-media monitoring cadence
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Communications team must activate, by 23:59 CET on May 28, 2026 (within 48 hours of the Rome launch), a structured 90-day earned-media and social-media monitoring programme tracking the terms 'Luce', 'Ferrari EV', 'LoveFrom', 'Cavallino Rampante', and 'Prancing Horse' across X, Instagram, Reddit (r/cars, r/ultracar), Italian-language news aggregators (ANSA, Corriere.it, IlSole24Ore.com), and Gulf Arabic-language automotive media, with sentiment scored weekly and reported to the CCO and Head of Brand; the programme must flag within 4 hours any post reaching more than 10,000 impressions or any statement from a named automotive analyst, journalist, or public official that could require a communications response. Given Oddo BHF analyst Anthony Dick's characterisation — reported on launch day — of this as the 'sharpest stock reaction ever seen for a car design', the monitoring cadence must produce a weekly dashboard comparing Luce sentiment trajectories against the Luce's first 90-day window and flagging any re-acceleration of negative volume ahead of Q4 2026 delivery commencement. Evidence of completion: monitoring platform activation confirmation by May 28, first weekly dashboard issued by June 4, and 90-day programme plan signed off by the CCO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a social and earned-media monitoring cadence; this adaptation specifies the exact search terms tied to the Luce and the LoveFrom design controversy, names the relevant Italian and Gulf media channels where UHNW audience sentiment is most commercially consequential, and adds a real-time escalation threshold anchored to the Oddo BHF analyst's publicly reported characterisation of launch-day severity.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Review Ferrari's investor-relations messaging to institutional shareholders —
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Investor Relations Director must, by June 9, 2026 (ahead of any Q2 2026 pre-close investor engagements), prepare and distribute a structured IR briefing document to Ferrari's top-20 institutional shareholders — including anchor shareholder Exor N.V. — that coherently explains three specific decisions whose rationale has been publicly questioned since the May 26 launch: (a) the decision to engage LoveFrom (Jony Ive's firm) as lead designer for the Luce and the governance process by which that mandate was approved, (b) the revision of Ferrari's EV-mix target from 40% to 20% of lineup by 2030, which was made at end of 2025 and must be reconciled with the $230M Maranello EV factory investment that opened in 2024, and (c) the EUR 550,000 price point and its relationship to Ferrari's stated strategy of volume exclusivity. The briefing must include a section addressing the approximately 6-8% share price decline on May 26 and must pre-empt the questions raised by Equita analysts' reported assessment that the Luce will not generate significant volumes capable of bolstering Ferrari's earnings. Evidence of completion: briefing document approved by the CFO and General Counsel, distribution log showing delivery to the top-20 shareholders by June 9, and read-receipt or acknowledgement from Exor N.V.'s investor-relations contact.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a review of investor-relations messaging; this adaptation makes it executable by naming the three specific decisions under institutional scrutiny — the LoveFrom mandate, the 40%-to-20% EV target revision, and the EUR 550,000 pricing logic — and anchoring the distribution obligation to the named anchor shareholder Exor N.V. and the named sell-side concerns from Equita.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Brief Ferrari's board on the reputational risk of
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Company Secretary must, by June 6, 2026, place the Luce reputational risk on the agenda of the next scheduled or special Ferrari board meeting, accompanied by a board paper prepared by the CCO and CFO that includes: (a) a factual summary of the May 26 launch-day events, including the approximately 6-8% Borsa Italiana share decline, Deputy Prime Minister Salvini's X post, former chairman Montezemolo's reported logo-removal suggestion, and Oddo BHF analyst Anthony Dick's reported characterisation of the launch as 'the sharpest design reaction ever measured for a car launch'; (b) a board-level narrative for use at the AGM and Q2 2026 results presentation that explains the strategic rationale for the Luce without dismissing or minimising the public criticism; and (c) a risk register entry for 'Luce brand-identity misalignment' rated at appropriate severity given a confirmed single-day share decline of 6-8% and publicly reported year-to-date decline of more than 32%. Evidence of completion: board agenda inclusion confirmation from the Company Secretary, board paper filed and distributed to directors, and the risk register entry signed off by the Chief Risk Officer.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a board briefing on reputational risk; this adaptation names the specific analyst (Oddo BHF's Dick), the confirmed financial impact (6-8% single-day decline on Borsa Italiana), and the two named public figures whose statements constitute the proximate reputational trigger, while tying the output to the specific upcoming governance events — AGM and Q2 2026 results — at which board-level narrative will be required.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Develop a contingency plan for the Luce brand
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Chief Commercial Officer must, by September 30, 2026 — prior to the commencement of Luce deliveries in Q4 2026 — complete a contingency brand plan for the Luce that is triggered if first-quarter delivery intake (Q4 2026) falls materially below the cadence implied by a sell-out of the production run; the plan must define 'material underperformance' with a specific numeric threshold agreed with the CFO (for example, fewer than X confirmed delivery orders as of October 31, 2026), and must evaluate at least three response options: (a) repositioning the Luce's communications away from the Prancing Horse sport-heritage narrative toward a luxury-lifestyle or sustainability narrative aimed at the prospective new-client cohort identified by CCO Galliera; (b) creating a separate Luce sub-brand or badge designation that reduces direct association with Ferrari's core sports-car identity, addressing the concern raised by Montezemolo's reported logo-removal suggestion; and (c) limiting Luce production and communications to a discrete collector's-edition framing that insulates Ferrari's core model range from volume-failure optics. Evidence of completion: contingency brand plan document approved by the CCO and CEO by September 30, 2026, with the numeric underperformance trigger confirmed in writing by the CFO.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a contingency plan for vehicle brand failure; this adaptation anchors the trigger to the confirmed Q4 2026 delivery start date, ties the repositioning options directly to the specific brand-identity criticism raised by Montezemolo and the new-client thesis publicly staked by Galliera, and requires a numeric threshold agreed between the CCO and CFO rather than leaving the activation criterion vague.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Deploy real-time social listening on the Luce hashtag
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Digital Communications team must confirm, by 23:59 CET on May 28, 2026 (within 48 hours of the Rome launch), that real-time social listening is active on at least the following tracked terms across X, Instagram, YouTube, and Italian-language news platforms: '#FerrrariLuce', '#Luce', 'LoveFrom', 'Jony Ive Ferrari', 'Cavallino Rampante', 'prancing horse', and 'Montezemolo Ferrari'; the listening dashboard must be configured to deliver immediate alerts (within 30 minutes) to the Head of Digital Communications and the CCO for any post or article exceeding 5,000 impressions or originating from a verified political figure, institutional investor, or automotive journalist. A written confirmation of channel coverage — specifying that X, Instagram, and Italian-language media are all actively monitored — must be signed off by the Head of Digital Communications and filed with the CCO as evidence of activation; any gap in coverage of these three channel categories must be escalated to the CCO within 24 hours of discovery.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires rapid social listening deployment; this adaptation specifies the exact search terms tied to the Luce and its named designers, names the three channel categories (X, Instagram, Italian-language media) where the launch-day backlash has already been confirmed to have originated, and sets an impressions-based escalation threshold that operationalises the control rather than leaving activation undefined.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Implement a structured counter-narrative campaign coordinating Ferrari CEO
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Communications Director must, by 17:00 CET on May 29, 2026 (within 72 hours of the Rome launch), deploy a coordinated counter-narrative package that aligns CEO Benedetto Vigna's 'new chapter for Ferrari' framing — used at the launch event — with the positive profitability analysis attributed to Banca Akros analysts, and that is distributed across at minimum: (a) a written statement issued through Ferrari's official Investor Relations newsroom referencing the EUR 550,000 price point as evidence of margin discipline; (b) a briefing provided to the top-five Italian and international automotive and financial journalists who covered the launch negatively, offering access to a Ferrari technical or commercial spokesperson; and (c) a curated social post on Ferrari's official Instagram and LinkedIn channels that foregrounds the Luce's exclusivity pricing without directly engaging Salvini's X post or Montezemolo's reported logo suggestion. The package must be reviewed and approved by both the CCO and the CFO before distribution to ensure financial claims are consistent with Ferrari's public guidance. Evidence of completion: CCO and CFO sign-off on the package, distribution confirmation for all three channels, and a media-pickup report filed by June 2, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires a counter-narrative campaign; this adaptation makes it executable by naming the specific positive analyst source (Banca Akros), the specific negative framing to be countered (Oddo BHF's 'design hate' characterisation), the exact three distribution channels with their audiences, and a 72-hour hard deadline that prevents the negative framing from solidifying before the weekend news cycle.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Scenario-plan three strategic outcomes for the Luce: (a)
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's Strategy function, led by the Chief Strategy Officer in coordination with the CFO, must produce a written three-scenario strategic plan for the Luce by July 26, 2026 (60 days post-launch), covering: (a) Full Commercial Success — Luce achieves sell-out delivery intake in Q4 2026, validating the LoveFrom design direction and the CCO's 50%-new-client thesis, with a defined threshold for what 'sell-out' means in unit and revenue terms given confirmed EUR 550,000 pricing; (b) Partial Demand Softness — Luce achieves between 40% and 80% of internal volume targets, requiring deployment of a follow-on sports-car EV variant using the $230M Maranello EV platform to demonstrate that the platform investment serves Ferrari's core performance identity, not only the Luce's lifestyle positioning; and (c) Volume Failure — Luce achieves fewer than 40% of internal volume targets, requiring the board to formally review whether to revise the 20%-EV-by-2030 target (itself already reduced from 40% at end of 2025) and to define a public communication strategy for that revision that does not further damage institutional investor confidence following the confirmed 6-8% share-price decline on May 26. Evidence of completion: scenario plan document approved by the CSO and CFO, presented to the board by July 31, 2026.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires scenario planning for a new product; this adaptation makes it executable by defining the numeric thresholds separating each scenario, anchoring scenario (b) specifically to the $230M Maranello platform as the strategic asset at risk, and grounding scenario (c) in the already-confirmed downward revision from 40% to 20% EV target — so the scenarios are not hypothetical but calibrated to Ferrari's publicly stated positions.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Evaluate whether Ferrari's disclosed commitment to develop and
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's CFO must commission, by June 2, 2026, an internal assessment — conducted by the Finance and Operations functions with input from the Maranello EV plant management team — of whether the $230M EV factory investment (opened 2024) constitutes a potential stranded-asset risk under IAS 36 (Impairment of Assets) in the event that Luce order intake over the 90-day window following Q4 2026 delivery commencement materially underperforms Ferrari's internal volume assumptions; the assessment must define the plant's break-even volume in Luce units per annum, calculate the headroom between break-even and the internal volume plan, and model a downside case in which the Luce achieves fewer than 50% of projected annual volumes — a scenario made plausible by Equita analysts' reported assessment that the Luce will not generate significant earnings-accretive volumes. The assessment must also evaluate whether a follow-on EV model on the same platform (consistent with scenario (b) in the STR-021 control) would materially change the impairment calculus. Findings and a recommendation on whether to initiate a formal IAS 36 impairment review must be presented to the Audit Committee by July 26, 2026. Evidence of completion: assessment document filed with the CFO by June 30, Audit Committee presentation by July 26.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires evaluation of stranded-asset risk for a manufacturing investment; this adaptation specifies the exact asset (the 2024 Maranello EV factory), the applicable accounting standard (IAS 36), the named sell-side source (Equita) whose reported assessment provides the revenue-risk foundation, and the Audit Committee as the governance destination — making the control both accountable and auditable.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Stress-test Ferrari's EUR 550
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's CFO must, by June 16, 2026, direct the Finance Planning team to complete a demand-sensitivity stress test on the EUR 550,000 Luce pricing assumption that models the impact on Ferrari's 2026 revenue guidance if the design backlash — evidenced by the confirmed approximately 6-8% Borsa Italiana share decline on May 26 and the Equita analysts' reported concern about volume generation — suppresses intake from the 50%-new-client cohort that CCO Enrico Galliera publicly projected; the stress test must include at minimum three intake scenarios (50%, 65%, and 80% of the internal volume plan attributable to new clients), must quantify the revenue shortfall in EUR terms under each scenario against the full-intake assumption, and must assess whether any material shortfall would require a public revision to 2026 guidance ahead of Q2 results. The output must be reviewed by the CFO and the Audit Committee Chair before the Q2 2026 results preparation cycle, with a recommendation on whether the sensitivity warrants disclosure in the Q2 interim report. Evidence of completion: stress-test model signed off by the CFO by June 16, Audit Committee Chair review confirmed, and disclosure recommendation documented.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires pricing stress-testing; this adaptation anchors the stress test to a specific public claim — Galliera's 50%-new-client thesis — and to the specific EUR 550,000 price point, names the three intake scenarios needed to bracket the risk, and ties the output explicitly to the Q2 2026 disclosure decision rather than leaving the commercial consequence undefined.

adaptive_controls_v2.parent_mc_title: Assess whether the $230M EV factory investment requires
adaptive_controls_v2.statement: Ferrari's CFO must, by August 24, 2026 (within 90 days of the May 26 Luce launch), initiate a preliminary IAS 36 impairment indicator assessment for the $230M Maranello EV factory investment (asset first recognised following the 2024 opening), using as the primary external impairment indicator the confirmed decline in Ferrari's Borsa Italiana-listed share price of approximately 6-8% on May 26, combined with the Equita analysts' reported assessment — to be treated as a corroborating market signal — that the Luce will not generate significant volumes capable of bolstering Ferrari's earnings; the assessment must determine whether the combination of the launch-day share reaction, the reported analyst volume scepticism, and the actual 90-day post-launch Luce order intake (measured from Q4 2026 delivery commencement) collectively constitute a triggering event under IAS 36 paragraph 12 that requires a full recoverable-amount calculation for the EV factory. If the 90-day order intake data is not yet available by the August 24 deadline, the CFO must document the status of the IAS 36 assessment and confirm a revised completion date not later than February 28, 2027 (after at least 90 days of delivery-period order data). Evidence of completion: signed IAS 36 indicator assessment memo filed with the Audit Committee by August 24, 2026, or a documented deferral rationale with a confirmed February 2027 completion date.
adaptive_controls_v2.rationale: The generic control requires consideration of an impairment review; this adaptation specifies the exact IAS 36 paragraph (12) that governs external impairment indicators, names the confirmed share-price decline as the triggering market signal, incorporates the Equita analyst assessment as a corroborating signal (appropriately treated as reported rather than confirmed), and provides a contingency completion date that accommodates the lag between launch and 90-day delivery order data.

incidents.headline: GKN Aerospace MMA tank hazmat emergency displaces 50,000 in California
incidents.summary: Beginning May 21, 2026, a 34,000-gallon storage tank at GKN Aerospace Transparency Systems in Garden Grove, California, containing approximately 7,000 gallons of highly toxic and flammable methyl methacrylate (MMA) began overheating and off-gassing, prompting California Governor Newsom to declare a state of emergency and forcing the evacuation of up to 50,000 Orange County residents. Throughout the May 26-27 window, roughly 16,000 residents remained unable to return home as crews worked to stabilise the tank; the BLEVE explosion threat was eliminated on May 25-26 after a crack relieved pressure, and all evacuation orders were finally lifted the evening of May 26. Parent company Melrose Industries saw shares fall as much as 7% on May 26, a class-action lawsuit was filed May 23, and the Orange County District Attorney launched a criminal investigation; the EPA and South Coast AQMD deployed air and storm-drain monitors with long-term environmental surveillance confirmed as ongoing.

secondary_mappings.why: Orange County DA launched a criminal investigation and OSHA/AQMD enforcement history is being scrutinised; EPA deployed coordinators and is conducting long-term environmental monitoring.
Up to 50,000 residents evacuated, some reporting respiratory symptoms from MMA vapour exposure; public health authorities issued guidance on exposure risks.
Melrose Industries (parent) shares fell up to 7% on May 26, class-action lawsuit filed, and congressional representatives publicly demanded the company take full responsibility.
The facility remains shuttered and under exclusion zone restrictions, directly impairing GKN Aerospace's production operations at the site.
A 22-year-old MMA storage tank failed due to overheating and multiple cracks, with prior OSHA and AQMD violations on record indicating chronic asset-integrity gaps.

incidents.severity_rationale: 50,000 residents displaced, state and federal emergency declarations issued, criminal investigation opened, parent-company share price dropped 7%, long-term environmental monitoring required, and prior compliance failures identified — a large-scale industrial pollution event with significant financial, regulatory, and community harm.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Ensure facilities storing reactive volatile chemicals (MMA) above EPCRA Tier II thresholds in California maintain continuously monitored, redundant cooling systems to prevent thermal runaway and uncontrolled off-gassing.

Ensure full and timely compliance with Cal/OSHA process-safety standards and EPA RMP obligations at all facilities with a documented prior violation history, including the Garden Grove site's 2018 citations.

Ensure the ~540 employees at Garden Grove and surrounding community residents have access to health monitoring and medical surveillance following confirmed MMA off-gassing exposure during the May 21-26 event.

Manage Melrose Industries' and GKN Aerospace's reputational exposure arising from the class-action filed May 23 and the OC District Attorney's criminal investigation through proactive, accurate stakeholder disclosure.
Maintain continuity of supply for military and commercial transparency products that the Garden Grove facility (£136M FY2025 revenue) can no longer produce during the indefinite shutdown.
Restore operational capability at the Garden Grove facility or an alternative site to meet existing military canopy and cockpit-window contracts suspended as of May 21, 2026.

Ensure the physical integrity and compliant design of all chemical storage and cooling infrastructure at GKN Aerospace facilities globally, in light of the single-point-of-failure cooling system identified at Garden Grove.
